# Fourier transform

| Fourier transforms |
| --- |
| * Fourier transform * [Fourier series](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fourier_series "Fourier series") * [Discrete-time Fourier transform](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Discrete-time_Fourier_transform "Discrete-time Fourier transform") * [Discrete Fourier transform](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Discrete_Fourier_transform "Discrete Fourier transform") * [Discrete Fourier transform over a ring](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Discrete_Fourier_transform_(general) "Discrete Fourier transform (general)") * [Fourier transform on finite groups](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fourier_transform_on_finite_groups "Fourier transform on finite groups") * [Fourier analysis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fourier_analysis "Fourier analysis") * [Related transforms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Fourier-related_transforms "List of Fourier-related transforms") |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/CQT-piano-chord.png/250px-CQT-piano-chord.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:CQT-piano-chord.png)

The Fourier transform applied to the waveform of a [C major](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/C_major "C major") [piano](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Piano "Piano") [chord](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chord_(music) "Chord (music)") (with logarithmic horizontal (frequency) axis). The first three peaks on the left correspond to the [fundamental frequencies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fundamental_frequency "Fundamental frequency") of the chord (C, E, G). The remaining smaller peaks are higher-frequency [overtones](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Overtone "Overtone") of the fundamental pitches.

In [mathematics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics"), the **Fourier transform** (**FT**) is an [integral transform](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Integral_transform "Integral transform") that takes a [function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Function_(mathematics) "Function (mathematics)") as input, and outputs another function that describes the extent to which various [frequencies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frequency "Frequency") are present in the original function. The output of the transform is a [complex valued function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_function "Complex function") of frequency. The term *Fourier transform* refers to both the [mathematical operation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Operation_(mathematics) "Operation (mathematics)") and to this complex-valued function. When a distinction needs to be made, the output of the operation is sometimes called the [frequency domain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frequency_domain "Frequency domain") representation of the original function. The Fourier transform is analogous to decomposing the [sound](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sound "Sound") of a musical [chord](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chord_(music) "Chord (music)") into the [intensities](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sound_intensity "Sound intensity") of its constituent [pitches](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pitch_(music) "Pitch (music)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Fourier_transform_time_and_frequency_domains_%28small%29.gif/250px-Fourier_transform_time_and_frequency_domains_%28small%29.gif)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Fourier_transform_time_and_frequency_domains_(small).gif)

The Fourier transform relates the time domain, in red, with a function in the domain of the frequency, in blue. The component frequencies, extended for the whole frequency spectrum, are shown as peaks in the domain of the frequency.

Functions that are localized in the time domain have Fourier transforms that are spread out across the frequency domain and vice versa, a phenomenon known as the [uncertainty principle](#Uncertainty_principle). The [critical](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Critical_point_(mathematics) "Critical point (mathematics)") case for this principle is the [Gaussian function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gaussian_function "Gaussian function"), of substantial importance in [probability theory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Probability_theory "Probability theory") and [statistics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Statistics "Statistics") as well as in the study of physical phenomena exhibiting [normal distribution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Normal_distribution "Normal distribution") (e.g., [diffusion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Diffusion "Diffusion")). The Fourier transform of a Gaussian function is another Gaussian function. [Joseph Fourier](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joseph_Fourier "Joseph Fourier") introduced [sine and cosine transforms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sine_and_cosine_transforms "Sine and cosine transforms") (which [correspond to the imaginary and real components](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sine_and_cosine_transforms#Relation_with_complex_exponentials "Sine and cosine transforms") of the modern Fourier transform) in his study of [heat transfer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heat_transfer "Heat transfer"), where Gaussian functions appear as solutions of the [heat equation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heat_equation "Heat equation").

The Fourier transform can be formally defined as an [improper](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Improper_integral "Improper integral") [Riemann integral](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Riemann_integral "Riemann integral"), making it an integral transform, although this definition is not suitable for many applications requiring a more sophisticated integration theory. For example, many relatively simple applications use the [Dirac delta function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dirac_delta_function "Dirac delta function"), which can be treated formally as if it were a function, but the justification requires a mathematically more sophisticated viewpoint.

The Fourier transform can also be generalized to functions of several variables on [Euclidean space](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Euclidean_space "Euclidean space"), sending a function of 3-dimensional "position space" to a function of 3-dimensional momentum (or a function of space and time to a function of [4-momentum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/4-momentum "4-momentum")). This idea makes the spatial Fourier transform very natural in the study of waves, as well as in [quantum mechanics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_mechanics "Quantum mechanics"), where it is important to be able to represent wave solutions as functions of either position or momentum and sometimes both. In general, functions to which Fourier methods are applicable are complex-valued, and possibly [vector-valued](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vector-valued_function "Vector-valued function"). Still further generalization is possible to functions on [groups](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Group_(mathematics) "Group (mathematics)"), which, besides the original Fourier transform on [**R**](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Real_number#Arithmetic "Real number") or **R***n*, notably includes the [discrete-time Fourier transform](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Discrete-time_Fourier_transform "Discrete-time Fourier transform") (DTFT, group = [**Z**](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Integers "Integers")), the [discrete Fourier transform](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Discrete_Fourier_transform "Discrete Fourier transform") (DFT, group = [**Z** mod *N*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cyclic_group "Cyclic group")) and the [Fourier series](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fourier_series "Fourier series") or circular Fourier transform (group = [*S*1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Circle_group "Circle group"), the unit circle вүҲ closed finite interval with endpoints identified). The latter is routinely employed to handle [periodic functions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Periodic_function "Periodic function"). The [fast Fourier transform](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fast_Fourier_transform "Fast Fourier transform") (FFT) is an algorithm for computing the DFT.

## Definition

The Fourier transform of a complex-valued function ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$ on the real line, is the complex valued function вҒ ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )}$вҒ , defined by the integral

Fourier transform

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)\ e^{-i2\pi \xi x}\,dx,\quad \forall \xi \in \mathbb {R} .}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)\ e^{-i2\pi \xi x}\,dx,\quad \forall \xi \in \mathbb {R} .}$ |  |  |

In this case ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$ is (Lebesgue) integrable over the whole real line, i.e., the above integral converges to a continuous function ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )}$ at all ${\displaystyle \xi }$${\displaystyle \xi }$ (decaying to zero as вҒ ${\displaystyle \xi \to \infty }$${\displaystyle \xi \to \infty }$вҒ ).

However, the Fourier transform can also be defined for (generalized) functions for which the [Lebesgue integral](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lebesgue_integral "Lebesgue integral") **[Eq.1](#math_Eq.1)** does not make sense. Interpreting the integral [suitably](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Weak_topology "Weak topology") (e.g. as an [improper integral](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Improper_integral "Improper integral") for [locally integrable](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Locally_integrable "Locally integrable") functions) extends the Fourier transform to functions that are not necessarily integrable over the whole real line. More generally, the Fourier transform also applies to [generalized functions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Generalized_function "Generalized function") like the [Dirac delta](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dirac_delta "Dirac delta") (and all other [tempered distributions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tempered_distributions "Tempered distributions")), in which case it is defined by duality rather than an integral.

First introduced in [Fourier's](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joseph_Fourier "Joseph Fourier") *Analytical Theory of Heat*., the corresponding inversion formula for "[sufficiently nice](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fourier_inversion_theorem#Conditions_on_the_function "Fourier inversion theorem")" functions is given by the [Fourier inversion theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fourier_inversion_theorem "Fourier inversion theorem"), i.e.,

Inverse transform

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| ${\displaystyle f(x)=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\widehat {f}}(\xi )\ e^{i2\pi \xi x}\,d\xi ,\quad \forall x\in \mathbb {R} .}$${\displaystyle f(x)=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\widehat {f}}(\xi )\ e^{i2\pi \xi x}\,d\xi ,\quad \forall x\in \mathbb {R} .}$ |  |  |

The functions ${\displaystyle f}$${\displaystyle f}$ and ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$ are referred to as a **Fourier transform pair**.В  A common notation for designating transform pairs is:
${\displaystyle f(x)\ {\stackrel {\mathcal {F}}{\longleftrightarrow }}\ {\widehat {f}}(\xi ).}$${\displaystyle f(x)\ {\stackrel {\mathcal {F}}{\longleftrightarrow }}\ {\widehat {f}}(\xi ).}$
For example, the Fourier transform of the delta function is the constant function вҒ ${\displaystyle 1}$${\displaystyle 1}$вҒ :
${\displaystyle \delta (x)\ {\stackrel {\mathcal {F}}{\longleftrightarrow }}\ 1.}$${\displaystyle \delta (x)\ {\stackrel {\mathcal {F}}{\longleftrightarrow }}\ 1.}$

### Angular frequency (*Пү*)

When the independent variable (вҒ ${\displaystyle x}$${\displaystyle x}$вҒ ) represents *time* (often denoted by вҒ ${\displaystyle t}$${\displaystyle t}$вҒ ), the transform variable (вҒ ${\displaystyle \xi }$${\displaystyle \xi }$вҒ ) represents [frequency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frequency "Frequency") (often denoted by вҒ ${\displaystyle f}$${\displaystyle f}$вҒ ). For example, if time has the unit [second](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second "Second"), then frequency has the unit [hertz](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hertz "Hertz"). The transform variable can also be written in terms of [angular frequency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Angular_frequency "Angular frequency"), вҒ ${\displaystyle \omega =2\pi \xi }$${\displaystyle \omega =2\pi \xi }$вҒ , with the unit [radian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radian "Radian") per second.

The substitution ${\displaystyle \xi ={\tfrac {\omega }{2\pi }}}$${\displaystyle \xi ={\tfrac {\omega }{2\pi }}}$ into **[Eq.1](#math_Eq.1)** produces this convention, where function ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$ is relabeled вҒ ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\_{1}}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\_{1}}$вҒ :
${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\widehat {f}}\_{3}(\omega )&\triangleq \int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)\cdot e^{-i\omega x}\,dx={\widehat {f}}\_{1}\left({\tfrac {\omega }{2\pi }}\right),\\f(x)&={\frac {1}{2\pi }}\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\widehat {f}}\_{3}(\omega )\cdot e^{i\omega x}\,d\omega .\end{aligned}}}$${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\widehat {f}}\_{3}(\omega )&\triangleq \int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)\cdot e^{-i\omega x}\,dx={\widehat {f}}\_{1}\left({\tfrac {\omega }{2\pi }}\right),\\f(x)&={\frac {1}{2\pi }}\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\widehat {f}}\_{3}(\omega )\cdot e^{i\omega x}\,d\omega .\end{aligned}}}$
Unlike the **[Eq.1](#math_Eq.1)** definition, the Fourier transform is no longer a [unitary transformation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unitary_transformation "Unitary transformation"), and there is less symmetry between the formulas for the transform and its inverse. Those properties are restored by splitting the ${\displaystyle 2\pi }$${\displaystyle 2\pi }$ factor evenly between the transform and its inverse, which leads to another convention:
${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\widehat {f}}\_{2}(\omega )&\triangleq {\frac {1}{\sqrt {2\pi }}}\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)\cdot e^{-i\omega x}\,dx={\frac {1}{\sqrt {2\pi }}}\ \ {\widehat {f}}\_{1}\left({\tfrac {\omega }{2\pi }}\right),\\f(x)&={\frac {1}{\sqrt {2\pi }}}\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\widehat {f}}\_{2}(\omega )\cdot e^{i\omega x}\,d\omega .\end{aligned}}}$${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\widehat {f}}\_{2}(\omega )&\triangleq {\frac {1}{\sqrt {2\pi }}}\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)\cdot e^{-i\omega x}\,dx={\frac {1}{\sqrt {2\pi }}}\ \ {\widehat {f}}\_{1}\left({\tfrac {\omega }{2\pi }}\right),\\f(x)&={\frac {1}{\sqrt {2\pi }}}\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\widehat {f}}\_{2}(\omega )\cdot e^{i\omega x}\,d\omega .\end{aligned}}}$
Variations of all three conventions can be created by conjugating the complex-exponential [kernel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Integral_kernel "Integral kernel") of both the forward and the reverse transform. The signs must be opposites.

Summary of popular forms of the Fourier transform, one-dimensional

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| ordinary frequency Оҫ (Hz) | unitary | ${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\widehat {f}}\_{1}(\xi )\ &\triangleq \ \int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)\,e^{-i2\pi \xi x}\,dx={\sqrt {2\pi }}\ \ {\widehat {f}}\_{2}(2\pi \xi )={\widehat {f}}\_{3}(2\pi \xi )\\f(x)&=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\widehat {f}}\_{1}(\xi )\,e^{i2\pi x\xi }\,d\xi \end{aligned}}}$${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\widehat {f}}\_{1}(\xi )\ &\triangleq \ \int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)\,e^{-i2\pi \xi x}\,dx={\sqrt {2\pi }}\ \ {\widehat {f}}\_{2}(2\pi \xi )={\widehat {f}}\_{3}(2\pi \xi )\\f(x)&=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\widehat {f}}\_{1}(\xi )\,e^{i2\pi x\xi }\,d\xi \end{aligned}}}$ |
| angular frequency Пү (rad/s) | unitary | ${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\widehat {f}}\_{2}(\omega )\ &\triangleq \ {\frac {1}{\sqrt {2\pi }}}\ \int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)\,e^{-i\omega x}\,dx={\frac {1}{\sqrt {2\pi }}}\ \ {\widehat {f}}\_{1}\!\left({\frac {\omega }{2\pi }}\right)={\frac {1}{\sqrt {2\pi }}}\ \ {\widehat {f}}\_{3}(\omega )\\f(x)&={\frac {1}{\sqrt {2\pi }}}\ \int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\widehat {f}}\_{2}(\omega )\,e^{i\omega x}\,d\omega \end{aligned}}}$${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\widehat {f}}\_{2}(\omega )\ &\triangleq \ {\frac {1}{\sqrt {2\pi }}}\ \int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)\,e^{-i\omega x}\,dx={\frac {1}{\sqrt {2\pi }}}\ \ {\widehat {f}}\_{1}\!\left({\frac {\omega }{2\pi }}\right)={\frac {1}{\sqrt {2\pi }}}\ \ {\widehat {f}}\_{3}(\omega )\\f(x)&={\frac {1}{\sqrt {2\pi }}}\ \int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\widehat {f}}\_{2}(\omega )\,e^{i\omega x}\,d\omega \end{aligned}}}$ |
| non-unitary | ${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\widehat {f}}\_{3}(\omega )\ &\triangleq \ \int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)\,e^{-i\omega x}\,dx={\widehat {f}}\_{1}\left({\frac {\omega }{2\pi }}\right)={\sqrt {2\pi }}\ \ {\widehat {f}}\_{2}(\omega )\\f(x)&={\frac {1}{2\pi }}\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\widehat {f}}\_{3}(\omega )\,e^{i\omega x}\,d\omega \end{aligned}}}$${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\widehat {f}}\_{3}(\omega )\ &\triangleq \ \int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)\,e^{-i\omega x}\,dx={\widehat {f}}\_{1}\left({\frac {\omega }{2\pi }}\right)={\sqrt {2\pi }}\ \ {\widehat {f}}\_{2}(\omega )\\f(x)&={\frac {1}{2\pi }}\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\widehat {f}}\_{3}(\omega )\,e^{i\omega x}\,d\omega \end{aligned}}}$ |

Generalization for *n*-dimensional functions

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| ordinary frequency Оҫ (Hz) | unitary | ${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\widehat {f}}\_{1}(\xi )\ &\triangleq \ \int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}f(x)e^{-i2\pi \xi \cdot x}\,dx=(2\pi )^{\frac {n}{2}}{\widehat {f}}\_{2}(2\pi \xi )={\widehat {f}}\_{3}(2\pi \xi )\\f(x)&=\int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}{\widehat {f}}\_{1}(\xi )e^{i2\pi \xi \cdot x}\,d\xi \end{aligned}}}$${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\widehat {f}}\_{1}(\xi )\ &\triangleq \ \int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}f(x)e^{-i2\pi \xi \cdot x}\,dx=(2\pi )^{\frac {n}{2}}{\widehat {f}}\_{2}(2\pi \xi )={\widehat {f}}\_{3}(2\pi \xi )\\f(x)&=\int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}{\widehat {f}}\_{1}(\xi )e^{i2\pi \xi \cdot x}\,d\xi \end{aligned}}}$ |

| non-unitary | ${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\widehat {f}}\_{3}(\omega )\ &\triangleq \ \int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}f(x)e^{-i\omega \cdot x}\,dx={\widehat {f}}\_{1}\left({\frac {\omega }{2\pi }}\right)=(2\pi )^{\frac {n}{2}}{\widehat {f}}\_{2}(\omega )\\f(x)&={\frac {1}{(2\pi )^{n}}}\int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}{\widehat {f}}\_{3}(\omega )e^{i\omega \cdot x}\,d\omega \end{aligned}}}$${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\widehat {f}}\_{3}(\omega )\ &\triangleq \ \int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}f(x)e^{-i\omega \cdot x}\,dx={\widehat {f}}\_{1}\left({\frac {\omega }{2\pi }}\right)=(2\pi )^{\frac {n}{2}}{\widehat {f}}\_{2}(\omega )\\f(x)&={\frac {1}{(2\pi )^{n}}}\int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}{\widehat {f}}\_{3}(\omega )e^{i\omega \cdot x}\,d\omega \end{aligned}}}$ |

### Lebesgue integrable functions

A [measurable function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Measurable_function "Measurable function") ${\displaystyle f:\mathbb {R} \to \mathbb {C} }$${\displaystyle f:\mathbb {R} \to \mathbb {C} }$ is called (Lebesgue) integrable if the [Lebesgue integral](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lebesgue_integral "Lebesgue integral") of its [absolute value](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Absolute_value "Absolute value") is finite:
${\displaystyle \|f\|\_{1}=\int \_{\mathbb {R} }|f(x)|\,dx<\infty .}$${\displaystyle \|f\|\_{1}=\int \_{\mathbb {R} }|f(x)|\,dx<\infty .}$
If ${\displaystyle f}$${\displaystyle f}$ is Lebesgue integrable then the Fourier transform, given by **[Eq.1](#math_Eq.1)**, is well-defined for all вҒ ${\displaystyle \xi \in \mathbb {R} }$${\displaystyle \xi \in \mathbb {R} }$вҒ . Furthermore, ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\in L^{\infty }\cap C\_{0}(\mathbb {R} )}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\in L^{\infty }\cap C\_{0}(\mathbb {R} )}$ is bounded, [uniformly continuous](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uniformly_continuous "Uniformly continuous") and (by the [RiemannвҖ“Lebesgue lemma](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Riemann%E2%80%93Lebesgue_lemma "RiemannвҖ“Lebesgue lemma")) [vanishing at infinity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vanish_at_infinity "Vanish at infinity"). Here ${\displaystyle C\_{0}(\mathbb {R} )}$${\displaystyle C\_{0}(\mathbb {R} )}$ denotes the space of continuous functions on ${\displaystyle \mathbb {R} }$${\displaystyle \mathbb {R} }$ that approach 0 as x approaches positive or negative infinity.

The space ${\displaystyle L^{1}(\mathbb {R} )}$${\displaystyle L^{1}(\mathbb {R} )}$ is the space of measurable functions for which the norm ${\displaystyle \|f\|\_{1}}$${\displaystyle \|f\|\_{1}}$ is finite, modulo the [equivalence relation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Equivalence_class "Equivalence class") of equality [almost everywhere](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Almost_everywhere "Almost everywhere"). The Fourier transform on ${\displaystyle L^{1}(\mathbb {R} )}$${\displaystyle L^{1}(\mathbb {R} )}$ is [one-to-one](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bijection,_injection_and_surjection "Bijection, injection and surjection"). However, there is no easy characterization of the image, and thus no easy characterization of the inverse transform. In particular, **[Eq.2](#math_Eq.2)** is no longer valid, as it was stated only under the hypothesis that ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$ was "sufficiently nice" (e.g., ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$ [decays with all derivatives](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Schwartz_function "Schwartz function")).

While **[Eq.1](#math_Eq.1)** defines the Fourier transform for (complex-valued) functions in вҒ ${\displaystyle L^{1}(\mathbb {R} )}$${\displaystyle L^{1}(\mathbb {R} )}$вҒ , it is not well-defined for other integrability classes, most importantly the space of [square-integrable functions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Square-integrable_function "Square-integrable function") вҒ ${\displaystyle L^{2}(\mathbb {R} )}$${\displaystyle L^{2}(\mathbb {R} )}$вҒ . For example, the function ${\displaystyle f(x)=(1+x^{2})^{-1/2}}$${\displaystyle f(x)=(1+x^{2})^{-1/2}}$ is in ${\displaystyle L^{2}}$${\displaystyle L^{2}}$ but not ${\displaystyle L^{1}}$${\displaystyle L^{1}}$ and therefore the Lebesgue integral **[Eq.1](#math_Eq.1)** does not exist. However, the Fourier transform on the dense subspace ${\displaystyle L^{1}\cap L^{2}(\mathbb {R} )\subset L^{2}(\mathbb {R} )}$${\displaystyle L^{1}\cap L^{2}(\mathbb {R} )\subset L^{2}(\mathbb {R} )}$ admits a unique continuous extension to a [unitary operator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unitary_operator "Unitary operator") on вҒ ${\displaystyle L^{2}(\mathbb {R} )}$${\displaystyle L^{2}(\mathbb {R} )}$вҒ . This extension is important in part because, unlike the case of вҒ ${\displaystyle L^{1}}$${\displaystyle L^{1}}$вҒ , the Fourier transform is an [automorphism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Automorphism "Automorphism") of the space вҒ ${\displaystyle L^{2}(\mathbb {R} )}$${\displaystyle L^{2}(\mathbb {R} )}$вҒ .

In such cases, the Fourier transform can be obtained explicitly by [regularizing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Regularization_(mathematics) "Regularization (mathematics)") the integral, and then passing to a limit. In practice, the integral is often regarded as an [improper integral](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Improper_integral "Improper integral") instead of a proper Lebesgue integral, but sometimes for convergence one needs to use [weak limit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Weak_limit "Weak limit") or [principal value](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cauchy_principal_value "Cauchy principal value") instead of the (pointwise) limits implicit in an improper integral. [Titchmarsh (1986)](#CITEREFTitchmarsh1986) and [Dym & McKean (1985)](#CITEREFDymMcKean1985) each gives three rigorous ways of extending the Fourier transform to square integrable functions using this procedure. A general principle in working with the ${\displaystyle L^{2}}$${\displaystyle L^{2}}$ Fourier transform is that Gaussians are dense in вҒ ${\displaystyle L^{1}\cap L^{2}}$${\displaystyle L^{1}\cap L^{2}}$вҒ , and the various features of the Fourier transform, such as its unitarity, are easily inferred for Gaussians. Many of the properties of the Fourier transform can then be proven from two facts about Gaussians:

* that ${\displaystyle e^{-\pi x^{2}}}$${\displaystyle e^{-\pi x^{2}}}$ is its own Fourier transform; and
* that the [Gaussian integral](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gaussian_integral "Gaussian integral") вҒ ${\displaystyle \textstyle \int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }e^{-\pi x^{2}}\,dx=1}$${\displaystyle \textstyle \int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }e^{-\pi x^{2}}\,dx=1}$вҒ .

A feature of the ${\displaystyle L^{1}}$${\displaystyle L^{1}}$ Fourier transform is that it is a homomorphism of Banach algebras from ${\displaystyle L^{1}}$${\displaystyle L^{1}}$ equipped with the convolution operation to the Banach algebra of continuous functions under the ${\displaystyle L^{\infty }}$${\displaystyle L^{\infty }}$ (supremum) norm. The conventions chosen in this article are those of [harmonic analysis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harmonic_analysis "Harmonic analysis"), and are characterized as the unique conventions such that the Fourier transform is both [unitary](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unitary_operator "Unitary operator") on вҒ ${\displaystyle L^{2}}$${\displaystyle L^{2}}$вҒ  and an algebra homomorphism from вҒ ${\displaystyle {1}}$${\displaystyle {1}}$вҒ  to вҒ ${\displaystyle L^{\infty }}$${\displaystyle L^{\infty }}$вҒ , without renormalizing the Lebesgue measure.

## Background

### History

In 1822, Fourier claimed (see *[Joseph Fourier В§В The Analytic Theory of Heat](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joseph_Fourier#The_Analytic_Theory_of_Heat "Joseph Fourier")*) that any function, whether continuous or discontinuous, can be expanded into a series of sines. That important work was corrected and expanded upon by others to provide the foundation for the various forms of the Fourier transform used since.

### Complex sinusoids

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Sine_voltage.svg/120px-Sine_voltage.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Sine_voltage.svg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Phase_shift.svg/250px-Phase_shift.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Phase_shift.svg)

The red [sinusoid](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sine_wave "Sine wave") can be described by peak amplitude (1), peak-to-peak (2), [RMS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Root_mean_square "Root mean square") (3), and [wavelength](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wavelength "Wavelength") (4). The red and blue sinusoids have a phase difference of Оё.

In general, the coefficients ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )}$ are complex numbers, which have two equivalent forms (see *[Euler's formula](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Euler%27s_formula "Euler's formula")*):
${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )=\underbrace {Ae^{i\theta }} \_{\text{polar coordinate form}}=\underbrace {A\cos(\theta )+iA\sin(\theta )} \_{\text{rectangular coordinate form}}.}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )=\underbrace {Ae^{i\theta }} \_{\text{polar coordinate form}}=\underbrace {A\cos(\theta )+iA\sin(\theta )} \_{\text{rectangular coordinate form}}.}$

The product with ${\displaystyle e^{i2\pi \xi x}}$${\displaystyle e^{i2\pi \xi x}}$ (**[Eq.2](#math_Eq.2)**) has these forms:
${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\widehat {f}}(\xi )\cdot e^{i2\pi \xi x}&=Ae^{i\theta }\cdot e^{i2\pi \xi x}\\[6pt]&=\underbrace {Ae^{i(2\pi \xi x+\theta )}} \_{\text{polar coordinate form}}\\[6pt]&=\underbrace {A\cos(2\pi \xi x+\theta )+iA\sin(2\pi \xi x+\theta )} \_{\text{rectangular coordinate form}},\end{aligned}}}$${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\widehat {f}}(\xi )\cdot e^{i2\pi \xi x}&=Ae^{i\theta }\cdot e^{i2\pi \xi x}\\[6pt]&=\underbrace {Ae^{i(2\pi \xi x+\theta )}} \_{\text{polar coordinate form}}\\[6pt]&=\underbrace {A\cos(2\pi \xi x+\theta )+iA\sin(2\pi \xi x+\theta )} \_{\text{rectangular coordinate form}},\end{aligned}}}$
which conveys both [amplitude](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amplitude "Amplitude") and [phase](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phase_offset "Phase offset") of frequency вҒ ${\displaystyle \xi }$${\displaystyle \xi }$вҒ . Likewise, the intuitive interpretation of **[Eq.1](#math_Eq.1)** is that multiplying ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$ by ${\displaystyle e^{-i2\pi \xi x}}$${\displaystyle e^{-i2\pi \xi x}}$ has the effect of subtracting ${\displaystyle \xi }$${\displaystyle \xi }$ from every frequency component of function вҒ ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$вҒ . Only the component that was at frequency ${\displaystyle \xi }$${\displaystyle \xi }$ can produce a non-zero value of the infinite integral, because (at least formally) all the other shifted components are oscillatory and integrate to zero (see *[В§В Example](#Example)*).

It is noteworthy how easily the product was simplified using the polar form, and how easily the rectangular form was deduced by an application of Euler's formula.

### Negative frequency

Euler's formula introduces the possibility of negative вҒ ${\displaystyle \xi }$${\displaystyle \xi }$вҒ . **[Eq.1](#math_Eq.1)** is defined вҒ ${\displaystyle \forall \xi \in \mathbb {R} }$${\displaystyle \forall \xi \in \mathbb {R} }$вҒ . Only certain complex-valued ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$ have transforms вҒ ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}=0,\ \forall \ \xi <0}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}=0,\ \forall \ \xi <0}$вҒ . (See *[Analytic signal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Analytic_signal "Analytic signal")*; a simple example is вҒ ${\displaystyle e^{i2\pi \xi \_{0}x}\ (\xi \_{0}>0)}$${\displaystyle e^{i2\pi \xi \_{0}x}\ (\xi \_{0}>0)}$вҒ .)В  But negative frequency is necessary to characterize all other complex-valued вҒ ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$вҒ , found in [signal processing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Signal_processing "Signal processing"), [partial differential equations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Partial_differential_equations "Partial differential equations"), [radar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radar "Radar"), [nonlinear optics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nonlinear_optics "Nonlinear optics"), [quantum mechanics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_mechanics "Quantum mechanics"), and others.

For a real-valued вҒ ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$вҒ , **[Eq.1](#math_Eq.1)** has the symmetry property ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(-\xi )={\widehat {f}}^{\*}(\xi )}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(-\xi )={\widehat {f}}^{\*}(\xi )}$ (see *[В§В Conjugation](#Conjugation)* below). This redundancy enables **[Eq.2](#math_Eq.2)** to distinguish ${\displaystyle f(x)=\cos(2\pi \xi \_{0}x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)=\cos(2\pi \xi \_{0}x)}$ from вҒ ${\displaystyle e^{i2\pi \xi \_{0}x}}$${\displaystyle e^{i2\pi \xi \_{0}x}}$вҒ . But it cannot determine the actual sign of вҒ ${\displaystyle \xi \_{0}}$${\displaystyle \xi \_{0}}$вҒ , because ${\displaystyle \cos(2\pi \xi \_{0}x)}$${\displaystyle \cos(2\pi \xi \_{0}x)}$ and ${\displaystyle \cos(2\pi (-\xi \_{0})x)}$${\displaystyle \cos(2\pi (-\xi \_{0})x)}$ are indistinguishable on just the real numbers line.

### Fourier transform for periodic functions

The Fourier transform of a periodic function cannot be defined using the integral formula directly. In order for integral in **[Eq.1](#math_Eq.1)** to be defined the function must be [absolutely integrable](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Absolutely_integrable_function "Absolutely integrable function"). Instead it is common to use [Fourier series](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fourier_series "Fourier series"). It is possible to extend the definition to include periodic functions by viewing them as [tempered distributions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Distribution_(mathematics)#Tempered_distributions "Distribution (mathematics)").

This makes it possible to see a connection between the [Fourier series](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fourier_series "Fourier series") and the Fourier transform for periodic functions that have a [convergent Fourier series](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Convergence_of_Fourier_series "Convergence of Fourier series"). If ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$ is a [periodic function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Periodic_function "Periodic function"), with period вҒ ${\displaystyle P}$${\displaystyle P}$вҒ , that has a convergent Fourier series, then:
${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )=\sum \_{n=-\infty }^{\infty }c\_{n}\cdot \delta \left(\xi -{\tfrac {n}{P}}\right),}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )=\sum \_{n=-\infty }^{\infty }c\_{n}\cdot \delta \left(\xi -{\tfrac {n}{P}}\right),}$
where ${\displaystyle c\_{n}}$${\displaystyle c\_{n}}$ are the Fourier series coefficients of вҒ ${\displaystyle f}$${\displaystyle f}$вҒ , and ${\displaystyle \delta }$${\displaystyle \delta }$ is the [Dirac delta function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dirac_delta_function "Dirac delta function"). In other words, the Fourier transform is a [Dirac comb](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dirac_comb "Dirac comb") function whose *teeth* are multiplied by the Fourier series coefficients.

### Sampling the Fourier transform

The Fourier transform of an [integrable](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Absolutely_integrable_function "Absolutely integrable function") function ${\displaystyle f}$${\displaystyle f}$ can be sampled at regular intervals of arbitrary length вҒ ${\displaystyle 1/P}$${\displaystyle 1/P}$вҒ . These samples can be deduced from one cycle of a periodic function вҒ ${\displaystyle f\_{P}}$${\displaystyle f\_{P}}$вҒ , which has [Fourier series](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fourier_series "Fourier series") coefficients proportional to those samples by the [Poisson summation formula](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poisson_summation_formula "Poisson summation formula"):
${\displaystyle f\_{P}(x)\triangleq \sum \_{n=-\infty }^{\infty }f(x+nP)={\frac {1}{P}}\sum \_{k=-\infty }^{\infty }{\widehat {f}}\left({\tfrac {k}{P}}\right)e^{i2\pi {\frac {k}{P}}x},\quad \forall k\in \mathbb {Z} .}$${\displaystyle f\_{P}(x)\triangleq \sum \_{n=-\infty }^{\infty }f(x+nP)={\frac {1}{P}}\sum \_{k=-\infty }^{\infty }{\widehat {f}}\left({\tfrac {k}{P}}\right)e^{i2\pi {\frac {k}{P}}x},\quad \forall k\in \mathbb {Z} .}$

The integrability of ${\displaystyle f}$${\displaystyle f}$ ensures the [periodic summation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Periodic_summation "Periodic summation") converges. Therefore, the samples ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}({\tfrac {k}{P}})}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}({\tfrac {k}{P}})}$ can be determined by Fourier series analysis:
${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\left({\tfrac {k}{P}}\right)=\int \_{P}f\_{P}(x)\cdot e^{-i2\pi {\frac {k}{P}}x}\,dx.}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\left({\tfrac {k}{P}}\right)=\int \_{P}f\_{P}(x)\cdot e^{-i2\pi {\frac {k}{P}}x}\,dx.}$

When ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$ has [compact support](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Compact_support "Compact support"), ${\displaystyle f\_{P}(x)}$${\displaystyle f\_{P}(x)}$ has a finite number of terms within the interval of integration. When ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$ does not have compact support, numerical evaluation of ${\displaystyle f\_{P}(x)}$${\displaystyle f\_{P}(x)}$ requires an approximation, such as tapering ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$ or truncating the number of terms.

## Units

The frequency variable must have inverse units to the units of the original function's domain (typically named ${\displaystyle t}$${\displaystyle t}$ or вҒ ${\displaystyle x}$${\displaystyle x}$вҒ ). For example, if ${\displaystyle t}$${\displaystyle t}$ is measured in seconds, ${\displaystyle \xi }$${\displaystyle \xi }$ should be in cycles per second or [hertz](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hertz "Hertz"). If the scale of time is in units of ${\displaystyle 2\pi }$${\displaystyle 2\pi }$ seconds, then another Greek letter ${\displaystyle \omega }$${\displaystyle \omega }$ is typically used instead to represent [angular frequency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Angular_frequency "Angular frequency") (where вҒ ${\displaystyle \omega =2\pi \xi }$${\displaystyle \omega =2\pi \xi }$вҒ ) in units of [radians](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radian "Radian") per second. If using ${\displaystyle x}$${\displaystyle x}$ for units of length, then ${\displaystyle \xi }$${\displaystyle \xi }$ must be in inverse length, e.g., [wavenumbers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wavenumber "Wavenumber"). That is to say, there are two versions of the real line: one that is the [range](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Range_of_a_function "Range of a function") of ${\displaystyle t}$${\displaystyle t}$ and measured in units of вҒ ${\displaystyle t}$${\displaystyle t}$вҒ , and the other that is the range of ${\displaystyle \xi }$${\displaystyle \xi }$ and measured in inverse units to the units of вҒ ${\displaystyle t}$${\displaystyle t}$вҒ . These two distinct versions of the real line cannot be equated with each other. Therefore, the Fourier transform goes from one space of functions to a different space of functions: functions that have a different domain of definition.

In general, ${\displaystyle \xi }$${\displaystyle \xi }$ must always be taken to be a [linear form](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linear_form "Linear form") on the space of its domain, which is to say that the second real line is the [dual space](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dual_space "Dual space") of the first real line. See the article *[Linear algebra](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linear_algebra "Linear algebra")* for a more formal explanation and for more details. This point of view becomes essential in generalizations of the Fourier transform to general [symmetry groups](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Symmetry_group "Symmetry group"), including the case of Fourier series.

That there is no one preferred way (often, one says "no canonical way") to compare the two versions of the real line that are involved in the Fourier transformвҖ”fixing the units on one line does not force the scale of the units on the other lineвҖ”is the reason for the plethora of rival conventions on the definition of the Fourier transform. The various definitions resulting from different choices of units differ by various constants.

In other conventions, the Fourier transform has i in the exponent instead of вҲ’*i*, and vice versa for the inversion formula. This convention is common in modern physics and is the default for [Wolfram Alpha](https://www.wolframalpha.com), and does not mean that the frequency has become negative, since there is no canonical definition of positivity for frequency of a complex wave. It simply means that ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )}$ is the amplitude of the waveВ вҒ ${\displaystyle e^{-i2\pi \xi x}}$${\displaystyle e^{-i2\pi \xi x}}$вҒ  instead of the waveВ ${\displaystyle e^{i2\pi \xi x}}$${\displaystyle e^{i2\pi \xi x}}$ (the former, with its minus sign, is often seen in the time dependence for [sinusoidal plane-wave solutions of the electromagnetic wave equation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sinusoidal_plane-wave_solutions_of_the_electromagnetic_wave_equation "Sinusoidal plane-wave solutions of the electromagnetic wave equation"), or in the [time dependence for quantum wave functions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wave_function#Time_dependence "Wave function")). Many of the identities involving the Fourier transform remain valid in those conventions, provided all terms that explicitly involve *i* have it replaced by вҲ’*i*. In [electrical engineering](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electrical_engineering "Electrical engineering") the letter *j* is typically used for the [imaginary unit](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Imaginary_unit "Imaginary unit") instead of *i* because *i* is used for current.

When using [dimensionless units](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dimensionless_units "Dimensionless units"), the constant factors might not be written in the transform definition. For instance, in [probability theory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Probability_theory "Probability theory"), the characteristic function ОҰ of the probability density function вҒ ${\displaystyle f}$${\displaystyle f}$вҒ  of a random variable вҒ ${\displaystyle X}$${\displaystyle X}$вҒ  of continuous type is defined without a negative sign in the exponential, and since the units of вҒ ${\displaystyle x}$${\displaystyle x}$вҒ  are ignored, there is no вҒ ${\displaystyle 2\pi }$${\displaystyle 2\pi }$вҒ  either:
${\displaystyle \varphi (\lambda )=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)e^{i\lambda x}\,dx.}$${\displaystyle \varphi (\lambda )=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)e^{i\lambda x}\,dx.}$

In probability theory and mathematical statistics, the use of the FourierвҖ”Stieltjes transform is preferred, because many random variables are not of continuous type, and do not possess a density function, and one must treat not functions but [distributions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Distribution_(mathematics) "Distribution (mathematics)"), i.e., measures that possess "atoms".

From the higher point of view of [group characters](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Character_theory "Character theory"), which is much more abstract, all these arbitrary choices disappear, as will be explained in the later section of this article, which treats the notion of the Fourier transform of a function on a [locally compact abelian group](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Locally_compact_abelian_group "Locally compact abelian group").

## Properties

Let ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$ and ${\displaystyle h(x)}$${\displaystyle h(x)}$ represent *integrable functions* [Lebesgue-measurable](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lebesgue-measurable "Lebesgue-measurable") on the real line satisfying:
${\displaystyle \int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }|f(x)|\,dx<\infty .}$${\displaystyle \int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }|f(x)|\,dx<\infty .}$
We denote the Fourier transforms of these functions as ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )}$ and ${\displaystyle {\widehat {h}}(\xi )}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {h}}(\xi )}$ respectively.

### Basic properties

The Fourier transform has the following basic properties:

#### Linearity

${\displaystyle a\ f(x)+b\ h(x)\ \ {\stackrel {\mathcal {F}}{\Longleftrightarrow }}\ \ a\ {\widehat {f}}(\xi )+b\ {\widehat {h}}(\xi );\quad \ a,b\in \mathbb {C} }$${\displaystyle a\ f(x)+b\ h(x)\ \ {\stackrel {\mathcal {F}}{\Longleftrightarrow }}\ \ a\ {\widehat {f}}(\xi )+b\ {\widehat {h}}(\xi );\quad \ a,b\in \mathbb {C} }$

#### Time shifting

${\displaystyle f(x-x\_{0})\ \ {\stackrel {\mathcal {F}}{\Longleftrightarrow }}\ \ e^{-i2\pi x\_{0}\xi }\ {\widehat {f}}(\xi );\quad \ x\_{0}\in \mathbb {R} }$${\displaystyle f(x-x\_{0})\ \ {\stackrel {\mathcal {F}}{\Longleftrightarrow }}\ \ e^{-i2\pi x\_{0}\xi }\ {\widehat {f}}(\xi );\quad \ x\_{0}\in \mathbb {R} }$

#### Frequency shifting

${\displaystyle e^{i2\pi \xi \_{0}x}f(x)\ \ {\stackrel {\mathcal {F}}{\Longleftrightarrow }}\ \ {\widehat {f}}(\xi -\xi \_{0});\quad \ \xi \_{0}\in \mathbb {R} }$${\displaystyle e^{i2\pi \xi \_{0}x}f(x)\ \ {\stackrel {\mathcal {F}}{\Longleftrightarrow }}\ \ {\widehat {f}}(\xi -\xi \_{0});\quad \ \xi \_{0}\in \mathbb {R} }$

#### Time scaling

${\displaystyle f(ax)\ \ {\stackrel {\mathcal {F}}{\Longleftrightarrow }}\ \ {\frac {1}{|a|}}{\widehat {f}}\left({\frac {\xi }{a}}\right);\quad \ a\neq 0}$${\displaystyle f(ax)\ \ {\stackrel {\mathcal {F}}{\Longleftrightarrow }}\ \ {\frac {1}{|a|}}{\widehat {f}}\left({\frac {\xi }{a}}\right);\quad \ a\neq 0}$
The case ${\displaystyle a=-1}$${\displaystyle a=-1}$ leads to the *time-reversal property*:
${\displaystyle f(-x)\ \ {\stackrel {\mathcal {F}}{\Longleftrightarrow }}\ \ {\widehat {f}}(-\xi )}$${\displaystyle f(-x)\ \ {\stackrel {\mathcal {F}}{\Longleftrightarrow }}\ \ {\widehat {f}}(-\xi )}$

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Fourier_unit_pulse.svg/330px-Fourier_unit_pulse.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Fourier_unit_pulse.svg "The transform of an even-symmetric real-valued function вҒ '\"`UNIQ--postMath-0000008D-QINU`\"'вҒ  is also an even-symmetric real-valued function (вҒ '\"`UNIQ--postMath-0000008E-QINU`\"'вҒ ). The time-shift, вҒ '\"`UNIQ--postMath-0000008F-QINU`\"'вҒ , creates an imaginary component, вҒ '\"`UNIQ--postMath-00000090-QINU`\"'вҒ . (See В§В Symmetry.)")

${\displaystyle \scriptstyle f(t)}$${\displaystyle \scriptstyle f(t)}$

${\displaystyle \scriptstyle {\widehat {f}}(\omega )}$${\displaystyle \scriptstyle {\widehat {f}}(\omega )}$

${\displaystyle \scriptstyle g(t)}$${\displaystyle \scriptstyle g(t)}$

${\displaystyle \scriptstyle {\widehat {g}}(\omega )}$${\displaystyle \scriptstyle {\widehat {g}}(\omega )}$

${\displaystyle \scriptstyle t}$${\displaystyle \scriptstyle t}$

${\displaystyle \scriptstyle \omega }$${\displaystyle \scriptstyle \omega }$

${\displaystyle \scriptstyle t}$${\displaystyle \scriptstyle t}$

${\displaystyle \scriptstyle \omega }$${\displaystyle \scriptstyle \omega }$

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Fourier_unit_pulse.svg/330px-Fourier_unit_pulse.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Fourier_unit_pulse.svg)

The transform of an even-symmetric real-valued function вҒ ${\displaystyle f(t)=f\_{\_{\text{RE}}}}$${\displaystyle f(t)=f\_{\_{\text{RE}}}}$вҒ  is also an even-symmetric real-valued function (вҒ ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{RE}}}}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{RE}}}}$вҒ ). The time-shift, вҒ ${\displaystyle g(t)=g\_{\_{\text{RE}}}+g\_{\_{\text{RO}}}}$${\displaystyle g(t)=g\_{\_{\text{RE}}}+g\_{\_{\text{RO}}}}$вҒ , creates an imaginary component, вҒ ${\displaystyle i\ {\widehat {g\ \!}}\_{\_{\text{IO}}}}$${\displaystyle i\ {\widehat {g\ \!}}\_{\_{\text{IO}}}}$вҒ . (See *[В§В Symmetry](#Symmetry)*.)

#### Symmetry

When the real and imaginary parts of a complex function are decomposed into their [even and odd parts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Even_and_odd_functions#EvenвҖ“odd_decomposition "Even and odd functions"), there are four components, denoted below by the subscripts RE, RO, IE, and IO. And there is a one-to-one mapping between the four components of a complex time function and the four components of its complex frequency transform:

:   ${\displaystyle {\begin{array}{rlcccccccc}{\mathsf {Time\ domain}}&f&=&f\_{\_{\text{RE}}}&+&f\_{\_{\text{RO}}}&+&i\ f\_{\_{\text{IE}}}&+&\underbrace {i\ f\_{\_{\text{IO}}}} \\&{\Bigg \Updownarrow }{\mathcal {F}}&&{\Bigg \Updownarrow }{\mathcal {F}}&&\ \ {\Bigg \Updownarrow }{\mathcal {F}}&&\ \ {\Bigg \Updownarrow }{\mathcal {F}}&&\ \ {\Bigg \Updownarrow }{\mathcal {F}}\\{\mathsf {Frequency\ domain}}&{\widehat {f}}&=&{\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{RE}}}&+&\overbrace {i\ {\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{IO}}}} &+&i\ {\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{IE}}}&+&{\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{RO}}}\end{array}}}$${\displaystyle {\begin{array}{rlcccccccc}{\mathsf {Time\ domain}}&f&=&f\_{\_{\text{RE}}}&+&f\_{\_{\text{RO}}}&+&i\ f\_{\_{\text{IE}}}&+&\underbrace {i\ f\_{\_{\text{IO}}}} \\&{\Bigg \Updownarrow }{\mathcal {F}}&&{\Bigg \Updownarrow }{\mathcal {F}}&&\ \ {\Bigg \Updownarrow }{\mathcal {F}}&&\ \ {\Bigg \Updownarrow }{\mathcal {F}}&&\ \ {\Bigg \Updownarrow }{\mathcal {F}}\\{\mathsf {Frequency\ domain}}&{\widehat {f}}&=&{\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{RE}}}&+&\overbrace {i\ {\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{IO}}}} &+&i\ {\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{IE}}}&+&{\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{RO}}}\end{array}}}$

From this, various relationships are apparent, for example:

* The transform of a real-valued function (вҒ ${\displaystyle f\_{\_{\text{RE}}}+f\_{\_{\text{RO}}}}$${\displaystyle f\_{\_{\text{RE}}}+f\_{\_{\text{RO}}}}$вҒ ) is the *[conjugate symmetric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Even_and_odd_functions#Complex-valued_functions "Even and odd functions")* function вҒ ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{RE}}}+i\ {\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{IO}}}}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{RE}}}+i\ {\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{IO}}}}$вҒ . Conversely, a *conjugate symmetric* transform implies a real-valued time-domain.
* The transform of an imaginary-valued function (вҒ ${\displaystyle i\ f\_{\_{\text{IE}}}+i\ f\_{\_{\text{IO}}}}$${\displaystyle i\ f\_{\_{\text{IE}}}+i\ f\_{\_{\text{IO}}}}$вҒ ) is the *[conjugate antisymmetric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Even_and_odd_functions#Complex-valued_functions "Even and odd functions")* function вҒ ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{RO}}}+i\ {\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{IE}}}}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{RO}}}+i\ {\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{IE}}}}$вҒ , and the converse is true.
* The transform of a *[conjugate symmetric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Even_and_odd_functions#Complex-valued_functions "Even and odd functions")* function ${\displaystyle (f\_{\_{\text{RE}}}+i\ f\_{\_{\text{IO}}})}$${\displaystyle (f\_{\_{\text{RE}}}+i\ f\_{\_{\text{IO}}})}$ is the real-valued function вҒ ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{RE}}}+{\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{RO}}}}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{RE}}}+{\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{RO}}}}$вҒ , and the converse is true.
* The transform of a *[conjugate antisymmetric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Even_and_odd_functions#Complex-valued_functions "Even and odd functions")* function ${\displaystyle (f\_{\_{\text{RO}}}+i\ f\_{\_{\text{IE}}})}$${\displaystyle (f\_{\_{\text{RO}}}+i\ f\_{\_{\text{IE}}})}$ is the imaginary-valued function вҒ ${\displaystyle i\ {\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{IE}}}+i\ {\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{IO}}}}$${\displaystyle i\ {\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{IE}}}+i\ {\widehat {f}}\!\_{\_{\text{IO}}}}$вҒ , and the converse is true.

#### Conjugation

${\displaystyle {\bigl (}f(x){\bigr )}^{\*}\ \ {\stackrel {\mathcal {F}}{\Longleftrightarrow }}\ \ \left({\widehat {f}}(-\xi )\right)^{\*}}$${\displaystyle {\bigl (}f(x){\bigr )}^{\*}\ \ {\stackrel {\mathcal {F}}{\Longleftrightarrow }}\ \ \left({\widehat {f}}(-\xi )\right)^{\*}}$
(Note: the вҒ ${\displaystyle \*}$${\displaystyle \*}$вҒ  denotes [complex conjugation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_conjugate "Complex conjugate").)

In particular, if ${\displaystyle f}$${\displaystyle f}$ is *real*, then ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$ is [conjugate symmetric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Even_and_odd_functions#Complex-valued_functions "Even and odd functions") (a.k.a. [Hermitian function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hermitian_function "Hermitian function")):
${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(-\xi )={\bigl (}{\widehat {f}}(\xi ){\bigr )}^{\*}.}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(-\xi )={\bigl (}{\widehat {f}}(\xi ){\bigr )}^{\*}.}$

If ${\displaystyle f}$${\displaystyle f}$ is purely imaginary, then ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$ is [odd symmetric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Even_and_odd_functions#Complex-valued_functions "Even and odd functions"):
${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(-\xi )=-({\widehat {f}}(\xi ))^{\*}.}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(-\xi )=-({\widehat {f}}(\xi ))^{\*}.}$

#### Real and imaginary parts

${\displaystyle \operatorname {Re} \{f(x)\}\ \ {\stackrel {\mathcal {F}}{\Longleftrightarrow }}\ \ {\tfrac {1}{2}}\left({\widehat {f}}(\xi )+{\bigl (}{\widehat {f}}(-\xi ){\bigr )}^{\*}\right)}$${\displaystyle \operatorname {Re} \{f(x)\}\ \ {\stackrel {\mathcal {F}}{\Longleftrightarrow }}\ \ {\tfrac {1}{2}}\left({\widehat {f}}(\xi )+{\bigl (}{\widehat {f}}(-\xi ){\bigr )}^{\*}\right)}$
${\displaystyle \operatorname {Im} \{f(x)\}\ \ {\stackrel {\mathcal {F}}{\Longleftrightarrow }}\ \ {\tfrac {1}{2i}}\left({\widehat {f}}(\xi )-{\bigl (}{\widehat {f}}(-\xi ){\bigr )}^{\*}\right)}$${\displaystyle \operatorname {Im} \{f(x)\}\ \ {\stackrel {\mathcal {F}}{\Longleftrightarrow }}\ \ {\tfrac {1}{2i}}\left({\widehat {f}}(\xi )-{\bigl (}{\widehat {f}}(-\xi ){\bigr )}^{\*}\right)}$

#### Zero frequency component

Substituting ${\displaystyle \xi =0}$${\displaystyle \xi =0}$ in the definition, we obtain:
${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(0)=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)\,dx.}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(0)=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)\,dx.}$

The integral of ${\displaystyle f}$${\displaystyle f}$ over its domain is known as the average value or [DC bias](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DC_bias "DC bias") of the function.

### Uniform continuity and the RiemannвҖ“Lebesgue lemma

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Rectangular_function.svg/250px-Rectangular_function.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Rectangular_function.svg)

The [rectangular function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rectangular_function "Rectangular function") is [Lebesgue integrable](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lebesgue_integrable "Lebesgue integrable").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Sinc_function_%28normalized%29.svg/250px-Sinc_function_%28normalized%29.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Sinc_function_(normalized).svg)

The [sinc function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sinc_function "Sinc function"), which is the Fourier transform of the rectangular function, is bounded and continuous, but not Lebesgue integrable.

The Fourier transform may be defined in some cases for non-integrable functions, but the Fourier transforms of integrable functions have several strong properties.

The Fourier transform ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$ of any integrable function ${\displaystyle f}$${\displaystyle f}$ is [uniformly continuous](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uniformly_continuous "Uniformly continuous") and
${\displaystyle \left\|{\widehat {f}}\right\|\_{\infty }\leq \left\|f\right\|\_{1}}$${\displaystyle \left\|{\widehat {f}}\right\|\_{\infty }\leq \left\|f\right\|\_{1}}$

By the *[RiemannвҖ“Lebesgue lemma](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Riemann%E2%80%93Lebesgue_lemma "RiemannвҖ“Lebesgue lemma")*,
${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )\to 0{\text{ as }}|\xi |\to \infty .}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )\to 0{\text{ as }}|\xi |\to \infty .}$

However, ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$ need not be integrable. For example, the Fourier transform of the [rectangular function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rectangular_function "Rectangular function"), which is integrable, is the [sinc function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sinc_function "Sinc function"), which is not [Lebesgue integrable](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lebesgue_integrable "Lebesgue integrable"), because its [improper integrals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Improper_integral "Improper integral") behave analogously to the [alternating harmonic series](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alternating_harmonic_series "Alternating harmonic series"), in converging to a sum without being [absolutely convergent](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Absolutely_convergent "Absolutely convergent").

It is not generally possible to write the *inverse transform* as a [Lebesgue integral](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lebesgue_integral "Lebesgue integral"). However, when both ${\displaystyle f}$${\displaystyle f}$ and ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$ are integrable, the inverse equality
${\displaystyle f(x)=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\widehat {f}}(\xi )e^{i2\pi x\xi }\,d\xi }$${\displaystyle f(x)=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\widehat {f}}(\xi )e^{i2\pi x\xi }\,d\xi }$ holds for almost every x. As a result, the Fourier transform is [injective](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Injective "Injective") on [*L*1(**R**)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lp_space "Lp space").

### Plancherel theorem and Parseval's theorem

Let вҒ ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$вҒ  and вҒ ${\displaystyle g(x)}$${\displaystyle g(x)}$вҒ  be integrable, and let вҒ ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$вҒ  and вҒ ${\displaystyle {\widehat {g}}}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {g}}}$вҒ  be their Fourier transforms. If вҒ ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$вҒ  and вҒ ${\displaystyle g(x)}$${\displaystyle g(x)}$вҒ  are also [square-integrable](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Square-integrable "Square-integrable"), then the Parseval formula follows:
${\displaystyle \langle f,g\rangle \_{L^{2}}=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x){\overline {g(x)}}\,dx=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\widehat {f}}(\xi ){\overline {{\widehat {g}}(\xi )}}\,d\xi ,}$${\displaystyle \langle f,g\rangle \_{L^{2}}=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x){\overline {g(x)}}\,dx=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\widehat {f}}(\xi ){\overline {{\widehat {g}}(\xi )}}\,d\xi ,}$
where the bar denotes [complex conjugation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_conjugation "Complex conjugation").

The [Plancherel theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plancherel_theorem "Plancherel theorem"), which follows from the above, states that
${\displaystyle \|f\|\_{L^{2}}^{2}=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }\left|f(x)\right|^{2}\,dx=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }\left|{\widehat {f}}(\xi )\right|^{2}\,d\xi .}$${\displaystyle \|f\|\_{L^{2}}^{2}=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }\left|f(x)\right|^{2}\,dx=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }\left|{\widehat {f}}(\xi )\right|^{2}\,d\xi .}$

Plancherel's theorem makes it possible to extend the Fourier transform, by a continuity argument, to a [unitary operator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unitary_operator "Unitary operator") on вҒ ${\displaystyle L^{2}(\mathbb {R} )}$${\displaystyle L^{2}(\mathbb {R} )}$вҒ . On вҒ ${\displaystyle L^{1}(\mathbb {R} )\cap L^{2}(\mathbb {R} )}$${\displaystyle L^{1}(\mathbb {R} )\cap L^{2}(\mathbb {R} )}$вҒ , this extension agrees with original Fourier transform defined on вҒ ${\displaystyle L^{1}(\mathbb {R} )}$${\displaystyle L^{1}(\mathbb {R} )}$вҒ , thus enlarging the domain of the Fourier transform to вҒ ${\displaystyle L^{1}(\mathbb {R} )+L^{2}(\mathbb {R} )}$${\displaystyle L^{1}(\mathbb {R} )+L^{2}(\mathbb {R} )}$вҒ  (and consequently to вҒ ${\displaystyle L^{p}(\mathbb {R} )}$${\displaystyle L^{p}(\mathbb {R} )}$вҒ  for вҒ ${\displaystyle 1\leq p\leq 2}$${\displaystyle 1\leq p\leq 2}$вҒ ). Plancherel's theorem has the interpretation in the sciences that the Fourier transform preserves the [energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Energy "Energy") of the original quantity. The terminology of these formulas is not quite standardised. [Parseval's theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parseval%27s_theorem "Parseval's theorem") was proved only for Fourier series, and was first proved by Lyapunov. But Parseval's formula makes sense for the Fourier transform as well, and so even though in the context of the Fourier transform it was proved by Plancherel, it is still often referred to as Parseval's formula, or Parseval's relation, or even Parseval's theorem.

See *[Pontryagin duality](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pontryagin_duality "Pontryagin duality")* for a general formulation of this concept in the context of locally compact abelian groups.

### Convolution theorem

The Fourier transform translates between [convolution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Convolution "Convolution") and multiplication of functions. If вҒ ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$вҒ  and вҒ ${\displaystyle g(x)}$${\displaystyle g(x)}$вҒ  are integrable functions with Fourier transforms вҒ ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$вҒ  and вҒ ${\displaystyle {\widehat {g}}(\xi )}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {g}}(\xi )}$вҒ  respectively, then the Fourier transform of the convolution is given by the product of the Fourier transforms вҒ ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$вҒ  and вҒ ${\displaystyle {\widehat {g}}}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {g}}}$вҒ  (under other conventions for the definition of the Fourier transform a constant factor may appear).

This means that if:
${\displaystyle h(x)=(f\*g)(x)=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(y)g(x-y)\,dy,}$${\displaystyle h(x)=(f\*g)(x)=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(y)g(x-y)\,dy,}$
where вҲ— denotes the convolution operation, then:
${\displaystyle {\widehat {h}}(\xi )={\widehat {f}}(\xi )\,{\widehat {g}}(\xi ).}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {h}}(\xi )={\widehat {f}}(\xi )\,{\widehat {g}}(\xi ).}$

In [linear time invariant (LTI) system theory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LTI_system_theory "LTI system theory"), it is common to interpret вҒ ${\displaystyle g(x)}$${\displaystyle g(x)}$вҒ  as the [impulse response](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Impulse_response "Impulse response") of an LTI system with input вҒ ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$вҒ  and output вҒ ${\displaystyle h(x)}$${\displaystyle h(x)}$вҒ , since substituting the [unit impulse](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dirac_delta_function "Dirac delta function") for вҒ ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$вҒ  yields вҒ ${\displaystyle h(x)=g(x)}$${\displaystyle h(x)=g(x)}$вҒ . In this case, вҒ ${\displaystyle {\widehat {g}}(\xi )}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {g}}(\xi )}$вҒ  represents the [frequency response](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Frequency_response "Frequency response") of the system.

Conversely, if вҒ ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$вҒ  can be decomposed as the product of two square integrable functions вҒ ${\displaystyle p(x)}$${\displaystyle p(x)}$вҒ  and вҒ ${\displaystyle q(x)}$${\displaystyle q(x)}$вҒ , then the Fourier transform of вҒ ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$вҒ  is given by the convolution of the respective Fourier transforms вҒ ${\displaystyle {\widehat {p}}(\xi )}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {p}}(\xi )}$вҒ  and вҒ ${\displaystyle {\widehat {q}}(\xi )}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {q}}(\xi )}$вҒ .

### Cross-correlation theorem

In an analogous manner, it can be shown that if вҒ ${\displaystyle h(x)}$${\displaystyle h(x)}$вҒ  is the [cross-correlation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cross-correlation "Cross-correlation") of вҒ ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$вҒ  and вҒ ${\displaystyle g(x)}$${\displaystyle g(x)}$вҒ :
${\displaystyle h(x)=(f\star g)(x)=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\overline {f(y)}}g(x+y)\,dy}$${\displaystyle h(x)=(f\star g)(x)=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\overline {f(y)}}g(x+y)\,dy}$
then the Fourier transform of вҒ ${\displaystyle h(x)}$${\displaystyle h(x)}$вҒ  is:
${\displaystyle {\widehat {h}}(\xi )={\overline {{\widehat {f}}(\xi )}}\,{\widehat {g}}(\xi ).}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {h}}(\xi )={\overline {{\widehat {f}}(\xi )}}\,{\widehat {g}}(\xi ).}$

As a special case, the [autocorrelation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Autocorrelation "Autocorrelation") of function вҒ ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$вҒ  is:
${\displaystyle h(x)=(f\star f)(x)=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\overline {f(y)}}f(x+y)\,dy}$${\displaystyle h(x)=(f\star f)(x)=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\overline {f(y)}}f(x+y)\,dy}$
for which
${\displaystyle {\widehat {h}}(\xi )={\overline {{\widehat {f}}(\xi )}}{\widehat {f}}(\xi )=\left|{\widehat {f}}(\xi )\right|^{2}.}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {h}}(\xi )={\overline {{\widehat {f}}(\xi )}}{\widehat {f}}(\xi )=\left|{\widehat {f}}(\xi )\right|^{2}.}$

### Differentiation

Suppose *f*(*x*) is differentiable [almost everywhere](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Almost_everywhere "Almost everywhere"), and both вҒ ${\displaystyle f}$${\displaystyle f}$вҒ  and its derivative вҒ ${\displaystyle f'}$${\displaystyle f'}$вҒ  are integrable (in вҒ ${\displaystyle L^{1}(\mathbb {R} )}$${\displaystyle L^{1}(\mathbb {R} )}$вҒ ). Then the Fourier transform of the derivative is given by
${\displaystyle {\widehat {f'}}(\xi )={\mathcal {F}}\left\{{\frac {d}{dx}}f(x)\right\}=i2\pi \xi {\widehat {f}}(\xi ).}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f'}}(\xi )={\mathcal {F}}\left\{{\frac {d}{dx}}f(x)\right\}=i2\pi \xi {\widehat {f}}(\xi ).}$
More generally, the Fourier transformation of the вҒ ${\displaystyle n}$${\displaystyle n}$вҒ th derivative вҒ ${\displaystyle f^{(n)}}$${\displaystyle f^{(n)}}$вҒ  is given by
${\displaystyle {\widehat {f^{(n)}}}(\xi )={\mathcal {F}}\left\{{\frac {d^{n}}{dx^{n}}}f(x)\right\}=(i2\pi \xi )^{n}{\widehat {f}}(\xi ).}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f^{(n)}}}(\xi )={\mathcal {F}}\left\{{\frac {d^{n}}{dx^{n}}}f(x)\right\}=(i2\pi \xi )^{n}{\widehat {f}}(\xi ).}$

Analogously, вҒ ${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}\left\{{\frac {d^{n}}{d\xi ^{n}}}{\widehat {f}}(\xi )\right\}=(i2\pi x)^{n}f(x)}$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}\left\{{\frac {d^{n}}{d\xi ^{n}}}{\widehat {f}}(\xi )\right\}=(i2\pi x)^{n}f(x)}$вҒ , so вҒ ${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}\left\{x^{n}f(x)\right\}=\left({\frac {i}{2\pi }}\right)^{n}{\frac {d^{n}}{d\xi ^{n}}}{\widehat {f}}(\xi )}$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}\left\{x^{n}f(x)\right\}=\left({\frac {i}{2\pi }}\right)^{n}{\frac {d^{n}}{d\xi ^{n}}}{\widehat {f}}(\xi )}$вҒ .

By applying the Fourier transform and using these formulas, some [ordinary differential equations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ordinary_differential_equation "Ordinary differential equation") can be transformed into algebraic equations, which are much easier to solve. These formulas also give rise to the [rule of thumb](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rule_of_thumb "Rule of thumb") "вҒ ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$вҒ  is smooth [if and only if](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/If_and_only_if "If and only if") вҒ ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )}$вҒ  quickly falls to вҒ ${\displaystyle 0}$${\displaystyle 0}$вҒ  for вҒ ${\displaystyle \vert \xi \vert \to \infty }$${\displaystyle \vert \xi \vert \to \infty }$вҒ ". By using the analogous rules for the inverse Fourier transform, one can also say "вҒ ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$вҒ  quickly falls to вҒ ${\displaystyle 0}$${\displaystyle 0}$вҒ  for вҒ ${\displaystyle \vert x\vert \to \infty }$${\displaystyle \vert x\vert \to \infty }$вҒ  if and only if вҒ ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )}$вҒ  is smooth."

### Eigenfunctions

The Fourier transform is a linear transform that has [eigenfunctions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eigenfunction "Eigenfunction") obeying вҒ ${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}[\psi ]=\lambda \psi }$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}[\psi ]=\lambda \psi }$вҒ , with вҒ ${\displaystyle \lambda \in \mathbb {C} }$${\displaystyle \lambda \in \mathbb {C} }$вҒ .

A set of eigenfunctions is found by noting that the homogeneous differential equation
${\displaystyle \left[U\left({\frac {1}{2\pi }}{\frac {d}{dx}}\right)+U(x)\right]\psi (x)=0}$${\displaystyle \left[U\left({\frac {1}{2\pi }}{\frac {d}{dx}}\right)+U(x)\right]\psi (x)=0}$
leads to eigenfunctions ${\displaystyle \psi (x)}$${\displaystyle \psi (x)}$ of the Fourier transform ${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}}$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}}$ as long as the form of the equation remains invariant under Fourier transform. In other words, every solution ${\displaystyle \psi (x)}$${\displaystyle \psi (x)}$ and its Fourier transform ${\displaystyle {\widehat {\psi }}(\xi )}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {\psi }}(\xi )}$ obey the same equation. Assuming [uniqueness](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ordinary_differential_equation#Existence_and_uniqueness_of_solutions "Ordinary differential equation") of the solutions, every solution ${\displaystyle \psi (x)}$${\displaystyle \psi (x)}$ must therefore be an eigenfunction of the Fourier transform. The form of the equation remains unchanged under Fourier transform if ${\displaystyle U(x)}$${\displaystyle U(x)}$ can be expanded in a power series in which for all terms the same factor of either one of вҒ ${\displaystyle \pm 1}$${\displaystyle \pm 1}$вҒ , вҒ ${\displaystyle \pm i}$${\displaystyle \pm i}$вҒ  arises from the factors ${\displaystyle i^{n}}$${\displaystyle i^{n}}$ introduced by the [differentiation](#Differentiation) rules upon Fourier transforming the homogeneous differential equation because this factor may then be cancelled. The simplest allowable ${\displaystyle U(x)=x}$${\displaystyle U(x)=x}$ leads to the [standard normal distribution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Normal_distribution#Fourier_transform_and_characteristic_function "Normal distribution").

More generally, a set of eigenfunctions is also found by noting that the [differentiation](#Differentiation) rules imply that the [ordinary differential equation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ordinary_differential_equation "Ordinary differential equation")
${\displaystyle \left[W\left({\frac {i}{2\pi }}{\frac {d}{dx}}\right)+W(x)\right]\psi (x)=C\psi (x)}$${\displaystyle \left[W\left({\frac {i}{2\pi }}{\frac {d}{dx}}\right)+W(x)\right]\psi (x)=C\psi (x)}$
with ${\displaystyle C}$${\displaystyle C}$ constant and ${\displaystyle W(x)}$${\displaystyle W(x)}$ being a non-constant even function remains invariant in form when applying the Fourier transform ${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}}$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}}$ to both sides of the equation. The simplest example is provided by вҒ ${\displaystyle W(x)=x^{2}}$${\displaystyle W(x)=x^{2}}$вҒ , which is equivalent to considering the SchrГ¶dinger equation for the [quantum harmonic oscillator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_harmonic_oscillator#Natural_length_and_energy_scales "Quantum harmonic oscillator"). The corresponding solutions provide an important choice of an orthonormal basis for [*L*2(**R**)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Square-integrable_function "Square-integrable function") and are given by the "physicist's" [Hermite functions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hermite_polynomials#Hermite_functions_as_eigenfunctions_of_the_Fourier_transform "Hermite polynomials"). Equivalently one may use
${\displaystyle \psi \_{n}(x)={\frac {\sqrt[{4}]{2}}{\sqrt {n!}}}e^{-\pi x^{2}}\mathrm {He} \_{n}\left(2x{\sqrt {\pi }}\right),}$${\displaystyle \psi \_{n}(x)={\frac {\sqrt[{4}]{2}}{\sqrt {n!}}}e^{-\pi x^{2}}\mathrm {He} \_{n}\left(2x{\sqrt {\pi }}\right),}$
where вҒ ${\displaystyle \mathrm {He} \_{n}(x)}$${\displaystyle \mathrm {He} \_{n}(x)}$вҒ  are the "probabilist's" [Hermite polynomials](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hermite_polynomial "Hermite polynomial"), defined as
${\displaystyle \mathrm {He} \_{n}(x)=(-1)^{n}e^{{\frac {1}{2}}x^{2}}\left({\frac {d}{dx}}\right)^{n}e^{-{\frac {1}{2}}x^{2}}.}$${\displaystyle \mathrm {He} \_{n}(x)=(-1)^{n}e^{{\frac {1}{2}}x^{2}}\left({\frac {d}{dx}}\right)^{n}e^{-{\frac {1}{2}}x^{2}}.}$

Under this convention for the Fourier transform, we have that
${\displaystyle {\widehat {\psi }}\_{n}(\xi )=(-i)^{n}\psi \_{n}(\xi ).}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {\psi }}\_{n}(\xi )=(-i)^{n}\psi \_{n}(\xi ).}$

In other words, the Hermite functions form a complete [orthonormal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Orthonormal "Orthonormal") system of [eigenfunctions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eigenfunctions "Eigenfunctions") for the Fourier transform on вҒ ${\displaystyle L^{2}(\mathbb {R} )}$${\displaystyle L^{2}(\mathbb {R} )}$вҒ . However, this choice of eigenfunctions is not unique. Because of ${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}^{4}=\mathrm {id} }$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}^{4}=\mathrm {id} }$ there are only four different [eigenvalues](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eigenvalue "Eigenvalue") of the Fourier transform (the fourth roots of unity вҒ ${\displaystyle \pm 1}$${\displaystyle \pm 1}$вҒ  and вҒ ${\displaystyle \pm i}$${\displaystyle \pm i}$вҒ ) and any linear combination of eigenfunctions with the same eigenvalue gives another eigenfunction. As a consequence of this, it is possible to decompose *L*2(**R**) as a direct sum of four spaces *H*0, *H*1, *H*2, and *H*3 where the Fourier transform acts on H*k* simply by multiplication by *i**k*.

Since the complete set of Hermite functions *ПҲn* provides a resolution of the identity they diagonalize the Fourier operator, i.e. the Fourier transform can be represented by such a sum of terms weighted by the above eigenvalues, and these sums can be explicitly summed:
${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}[f](\xi )=\int dxf(x)\sum \_{n\geq 0}(-i)^{n}\psi \_{n}(x)\psi \_{n}(\xi )~.}$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}[f](\xi )=\int dxf(x)\sum \_{n\geq 0}(-i)^{n}\psi \_{n}(x)\psi \_{n}(\xi )~.}$

This approach to define the Fourier transform was first proposed by [Norbert Wiener](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Norbert_Wiener "Norbert Wiener"). Among other properties, Hermite functions decrease exponentially fast in both frequency and time domains, and they are thus used to define a generalization of the Fourier transform, namely the [fractional Fourier transform](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractional_Fourier_transform "Fractional Fourier transform") used in timeвҖ“frequency analysis. In [physics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Physics "Physics"), this transform was introduced by [Edward Condon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Edward_Condon "Edward Condon"). This [change of basis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Change_of_basis "Change of basis") becomes possible because the Fourier transform is a unitary transform when using the right [conventions](#Other_conventions). Consequently, under the proper conditions it may be expected to result from a self-adjoint generator ${\displaystyle N}$${\displaystyle N}$ via
${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}[\psi ]=e^{-itN}\psi .}$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}[\psi ]=e^{-itN}\psi .}$

The operator ${\displaystyle N}$${\displaystyle N}$ is the [number operator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_harmonic_oscillator#Ladder_operator_method "Quantum harmonic oscillator") of the quantum harmonic oscillator written as
${\displaystyle N\equiv {\frac {1}{2}}\left(x-{\frac {\partial }{\partial x}}\right)\left(x+{\frac {\partial }{\partial x}}\right)={\frac {1}{2}}\left(-{\frac {\partial ^{2}}{\partial x^{2}}}+x^{2}-1\right).}$${\displaystyle N\equiv {\frac {1}{2}}\left(x-{\frac {\partial }{\partial x}}\right)\left(x+{\frac {\partial }{\partial x}}\right)={\frac {1}{2}}\left(-{\frac {\partial ^{2}}{\partial x^{2}}}+x^{2}-1\right).}$

It can be interpreted as the [generator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Symmetry_in_quantum_mechanics "Symmetry in quantum mechanics") of [fractional Fourier transforms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mehler_kernel#Fractional_Fourier_transform "Mehler kernel") for arbitrary values of t, and of the conventional continuous Fourier transform ${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}}$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}}$ for the particular value вҒ ${\displaystyle t=\pi /2}$${\displaystyle t=\pi /2}$вҒ , with the [Mehler kernel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mehler_kernel#Physics_version "Mehler kernel") implementing the corresponding [active transform](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Active_and_passive_transformation#In_abstract_vector_spaces "Active and passive transformation"). The eigenfunctions of ${\displaystyle N}$${\displaystyle N}$ are the [Hermite functions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hermite_polynomials#Hermite_functions "Hermite polynomials") вҒ ${\displaystyle \psi \_{n}(x)}$${\displaystyle \psi \_{n}(x)}$вҒ , which are therefore also eigenfunctions of вҒ ${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}}$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}}$вҒ .

Upon extending the Fourier transform to [distributions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Distribution_(mathematics) "Distribution (mathematics)") the [Dirac comb](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dirac_comb#Fourier_transform "Dirac comb") is also an eigenfunction of the Fourier transform.

### Inversion and periodicity

Under suitable conditions on the function вҒ ${\displaystyle f}$${\displaystyle f}$вҒ , it can be recovered from its Fourier transform вҒ ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$вҒ . Indeed, denoting the Fourier transform operator by вҒ ${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}}$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}}$вҒ , so вҒ ${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}f:={\widehat {f}}}$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}f:={\widehat {f}}}$вҒ , then for suitable functions, applying the Fourier transform twice simply flips the function: вҒ ${\displaystyle \left({\mathcal {F}}^{2}f\right)(x)=f(-x)}$${\displaystyle \left({\mathcal {F}}^{2}f\right)(x)=f(-x)}$вҒ , which can be interpreted as "reversing time". Since reversing time is two-periodic, applying this twice yields вҒ ${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}^{4}(f)=f}$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}^{4}(f)=f}$вҒ , so the Fourier transform operator is four-periodic, and similarly the inverse Fourier transform can be obtained by applying the Fourier transform three times: вҒ ${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}^{3}\left({\widehat {f}}\right)=f}$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}^{3}\left({\widehat {f}}\right)=f}$вҒ . In particular the Fourier transform is invertible (under suitable conditions).

More precisely, defining the *parity operator* ${\displaystyle {\mathcal {P}}}$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {P}}}$ such that вҒ ${\displaystyle ({\mathcal {P}}f)(x)=f(-x)}$${\displaystyle ({\mathcal {P}}f)(x)=f(-x)}$вҒ , we have:
${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\mathcal {F}}^{0}&=\mathrm {id} ,\\{\mathcal {F}}^{1}&={\mathcal {F}},\\{\mathcal {F}}^{2}&={\mathcal {P}},\\{\mathcal {F}}^{3}&={\mathcal {F}}^{-1}={\mathcal {P}}\circ {\mathcal {F}}={\mathcal {F}}\circ {\mathcal {P}},\\{\mathcal {F}}^{4}&=\mathrm {id} \end{aligned}}}$${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\mathcal {F}}^{0}&=\mathrm {id} ,\\{\mathcal {F}}^{1}&={\mathcal {F}},\\{\mathcal {F}}^{2}&={\mathcal {P}},\\{\mathcal {F}}^{3}&={\mathcal {F}}^{-1}={\mathcal {P}}\circ {\mathcal {F}}={\mathcal {F}}\circ {\mathcal {P}},\\{\mathcal {F}}^{4}&=\mathrm {id} \end{aligned}}}$
These equalities of operators require careful definition of the space of functions in question, defining equality of functions (equality at every point? equality [almost everywhere](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Almost_everywhere "Almost everywhere")?) and defining equality of operators вҖ“ that is, defining the topology on the function space and operator space in question. These are not true for all functions, but are true under various conditions, which are the content of the various forms of the [Fourier inversion theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fourier_inversion_theorem "Fourier inversion theorem").

This fourfold periodicity of the Fourier transform is similar to a rotation of the plane by 90В°, particularly as the two-fold iteration yields a reversal, and in fact this analogy can be made precise. While the Fourier transform can simply be interpreted as switching the time domain and the frequency domain, with the inverse Fourier transform switching them back, more geometrically it can be interpreted as a rotation by 90В° in the [timeвҖ“frequency domain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Time%E2%80%93frequency_domain "TimeвҖ“frequency domain") (considering time as the вҒ ${\displaystyle x}$${\displaystyle x}$вҒ -axis and frequency as the вҒ ${\displaystyle y}$${\displaystyle y}$вҒ -axis), and the Fourier transform can be generalized to the [fractional Fourier transform](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractional_Fourier_transform "Fractional Fourier transform"), which involves rotations by other angles. This can be further generalized to [linear canonical transformations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linear_canonical_transformation "Linear canonical transformation"), which can be visualized as the action of the [special linear group](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Special_linear_group "Special linear group") [SL2(**R**)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SL2(R) "SL2(R)") on the timeвҖ“frequency plane, with the preserved symplectic form corresponding to the [uncertainty principle](#Uncertainty_principle), below. This approach is particularly studied in [signal processing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Signal_processing "Signal processing"), under [timeвҖ“frequency analysis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Time%E2%80%93frequency_analysis "TimeвҖ“frequency analysis").

### Connection with the Heisenberg group
