The [Heisenberg group](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heisenberg_group "Heisenberg group") is a certain [group](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Group_(mathematics) "Group (mathematics)") of [unitary operators](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unitary_operator "Unitary operator") on the [Hilbert space](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hilbert_space "Hilbert space") *L*2(**R**) of square integrable complex valued functions f on the real line, generated by the translations (*Ty f*)(*x*) = *f* (*x* + *y*) and multiplication by *e**i*2ПҖ*Оҫx*, (*MОҫ f*)(*x*) = *e**i*2ПҖ*Оҫx* *f* (*x*). These operators do not commute, as their (group) commutator is
${\displaystyle \left(M\_{\xi }^{-1}T\_{y}^{-1}M\_{\xi }T\_{y}f\right)(x)=e^{i2\pi \xi y}f(x),}$${\displaystyle \left(M\_{\xi }^{-1}T\_{y}^{-1}M\_{\xi }T\_{y}f\right)(x)=e^{i2\pi \xi y}f(x),}$
which is multiplication by the constant (independent of x) *e**i*2ПҖ*Оҫy* вҲҲ *U*(1) (the [circle group](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Circle_group "Circle group") of unit modulus complex numbers). As an abstract group, the Heisenberg group is the three-dimensional [Lie group](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lie_group "Lie group") of triples (*x*, *Оҫ*, *z*) вҲҲ **R**2 Г— *U*(1), with the group law
${\displaystyle \left(x\_{1},\xi \_{1},t\_{1}\right)\cdot \left(x\_{2},\xi \_{2},t\_{2}\right)=\left(x\_{1}+x\_{2},\xi \_{1}+\xi \_{2},t\_{1}t\_{2}e^{-2i\pi x\_{1}\xi \_{2}}\right).}$${\displaystyle \left(x\_{1},\xi \_{1},t\_{1}\right)\cdot \left(x\_{2},\xi \_{2},t\_{2}\right)=\left(x\_{1}+x\_{2},\xi \_{1}+\xi \_{2},t\_{1}t\_{2}e^{-2i\pi x\_{1}\xi \_{2}}\right).}$

Denote the Heisenberg group by *H*1. The above procedure describes not only the group structure, but also a standard [unitary representation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unitary_representation "Unitary representation") of *H*1 on a Hilbert space, which we denote by *ПҒ*В : *H*1 вҶ’ *B*(*L*2(**R**)). Define the linear automorphism of **R**2 by
${\displaystyle J{\begin{pmatrix}x\\\xi \end{pmatrix}}={\begin{pmatrix}-\xi \\x\end{pmatrix}}}$${\displaystyle J{\begin{pmatrix}x\\\xi \end{pmatrix}}={\begin{pmatrix}-\xi \\x\end{pmatrix}}}$
so that *J*2 = вҲ’*I*. This J can be extended to a unique automorphism of *H*1:
${\displaystyle j\left(x,\xi ,t\right)=\left(-\xi ,x,te^{-i2\pi \xi x}\right).}$${\displaystyle j\left(x,\xi ,t\right)=\left(-\xi ,x,te^{-i2\pi \xi x}\right).}$

According to the [StoneвҖ“von Neumann theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stone%E2%80%93von_Neumann_theorem "StoneвҖ“von Neumann theorem"), the unitary representations ПҒ and *ПҒ* вҲҳ *j* are unitarily equivalent, so there is a unique intertwiner *W* вҲҲ *U*(*L*2(**R**)) such that
${\displaystyle \rho \circ j=W\rho W^{\*}.}$${\displaystyle \rho \circ j=W\rho W^{\*}.}$
This operator W is the Fourier transform.

Many of the standard properties of the Fourier transform are immediate consequences of this more general framework. For example, the square of the Fourier transform, *W*2, is an intertwiner associated with *J*2 = вҲ’*I*, and so we have (*W*2*f*)(*x*) = *f* (вҲ’*x*) is the reflection of the original function f.

## Complex domain

The [integral](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Integral "Integral") for the Fourier transform
${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }e^{-i2\pi \xi t}f(t)\,dt}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }e^{-i2\pi \xi t}f(t)\,dt}$
can be studied for [complex](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_number "Complex number") values of its argument Оҫ. Depending on the properties of f, this might not converge off the real axis at all, or it might converge to a [complex](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_analysis "Complex analysis") [analytic function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Analytic_function "Analytic function") for all values of *Оҫ* = *Пғ* + *iП„*, or something in between.

The [PaleyвҖ“Wiener theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paley%E2%80%93Wiener_theorem "PaleyвҖ“Wiener theorem") says that f is smooth (i.e., n-times differentiable for all positive integers n) and compactly supported if and only if *fМӮ* (*Пғ* + *iП„*) is a [holomorphic function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Holomorphic_function "Holomorphic function") for which there exists a [constant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Constant_(mathematics) "Constant (mathematics)") *a* > 0 such that for any [integer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Integer "Integer") *n* вүҘ 0,
${\displaystyle \left\vert \xi ^{n}{\widehat {f}}(\xi )\right\vert \leq Ce^{a\vert \tau \vert }}$${\displaystyle \left\vert \xi ^{n}{\widehat {f}}(\xi )\right\vert \leq Ce^{a\vert \tau \vert }}$
for some constant C. (In this case, f is supported on [вҲ’*a*, *a*].) This can be expressed by saying that *fМӮ* is an [entire function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Entire_function "Entire function") that is [rapidly decreasing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rapidly_decreasing "Rapidly decreasing") in Пғ (for fixed П„) and of exponential growth in П„ (uniformly in Пғ).

(If f is not smooth, but only *L*2, the statement still holds provided *n* = 0.) The space of such functions of a [complex variable](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_analysis "Complex analysis") is called the PaleyвҖ“Wiener space. This theorem has been generalised to semisimple [Lie groups](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lie_group "Lie group").

If f is supported on the half-line *t* вүҘ 0, then f is said to be "causal" because the [impulse response function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Impulse_response_function "Impulse response function") of a physically realisable [filter](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Filter_(mathematics) "Filter (mathematics)") must have this property, as no effect can precede its cause. [Paley](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Raymond_Paley "Raymond Paley") and Wiener showed that then *fМӮ* extends to a [holomorphic function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Holomorphic_function "Holomorphic function") on the complex lower half-plane *П„* < 0 that tends to zero as П„ goes to infinity. The converse is false and it is not known how to characterise the Fourier transform of a causal function.

### Laplace transform

The Fourier transform *fМӮ*(*Оҫ*) is related to the [Laplace transform](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Laplace_transform "Laplace transform") *F*(*s*), which is also used for the solution of [differential equations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Differential_equation "Differential equation") and the analysis of [filters](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Filter_(signal_processing) "Filter (signal processing)").

It may happen that a function f for which the Fourier integral does not converge on the real axis at all, nevertheless has a complex Fourier transform defined in some region of the [complex plane](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complex_plane "Complex plane").

For example, if *f*(*t*) is of exponential growth, i.e.,
${\displaystyle \vert f(t)\vert <Ce^{a\vert t\vert }}$${\displaystyle \vert f(t)\vert <Ce^{a\vert t\vert }}$
for some constants *C*, *a* вүҘ 0, then
${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(i\tau )=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }e^{2\pi \tau t}f(t)\,dt,}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(i\tau )=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }e^{2\pi \tau t}f(t)\,dt,}$
convergent for all 2ПҖ*П„* < вҲ’*a*, is the [two-sided Laplace transform](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Two-sided_Laplace_transform "Two-sided Laplace transform") of f.

The more usual version ("one-sided") of the Laplace transform is
${\displaystyle F(s)=\int \_{0}^{\infty }f(t)e^{-st}\,dt.}$${\displaystyle F(s)=\int \_{0}^{\infty }f(t)e^{-st}\,dt.}$

If f is also causal and analytic, then: вҒ ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(i\tau )=F(-2\pi \tau )}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(i\tau )=F(-2\pi \tau )}$вҒ . Thus, extending the Fourier transform to the complex domain means it includes the Laplace transform as a special case in the case of causal functionsвҖ”but with the change of variable *s* = *i*2ПҖ*Оҫ*.

From another, perhaps more classical viewpoint, the Laplace transform by its form involves an additional exponential regulating term that lets it converge outside of the imaginary line where the Fourier transform is defined. As such it can converge for at most exponentially divergent series and integrals, whereas the original Fourier decomposition cannot, enabling analysis of systems with divergent or critical elements. Two particular examples from linear signal processing are the construction of allpass filter networks from critical comb and mitigating filters via exact pole-zero cancellation on the unit circle. Such designs are common in audio processing, where highly nonlinear [phase response](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phase_response "Phase response") is sought for, as in reverb.

Furthermore, when extended pulselike impulse responses are sought for signal processing work, the easiest way to produce them is to have one circuit that produces a divergent time response, and then to cancel its divergence through a delayed opposite and compensatory response. There, only the delay circuit in-between admits a classical Fourier description, which is critical. Both the circuits to the side are unstable, and do not admit a convergent Fourier decomposition. However, they do admit a Laplace domain description, with identical half-planes of convergence in the complex plane (or in the discrete case, the Z-plane), wherein their effects cancel.

In modern mathematics the Laplace transform is conventionally subsumed under the aegis Fourier methods. Both of them are subsumed by the far more general, and more abstract, idea of [harmonic analysis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harmonic_analysis "Harmonic analysis").

### Inversion

Still with вҒ ${\displaystyle \xi =\sigma +i\tau }$${\displaystyle \xi =\sigma +i\tau }$вҒ , if ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$ is complex analytic for *a* вүӨ *П„* вүӨ *b*, then
${\displaystyle \int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\widehat {f}}(\sigma +ia)e^{i2\pi \xi t}\,d\sigma =\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\widehat {f}}(\sigma +ib)e^{i2\pi \xi t}\,d\sigma }$${\displaystyle \int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\widehat {f}}(\sigma +ia)e^{i2\pi \xi t}\,d\sigma =\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\widehat {f}}(\sigma +ib)e^{i2\pi \xi t}\,d\sigma }$
by [Cauchy's integral theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cauchy%27s_integral_theorem "Cauchy's integral theorem"). Therefore, the Fourier inversion formula can use integration along different lines, parallel to the real axis.

Theorem: If *f*(*t*) = 0 for *t* < 0, and |*f*(*t*)| < *Ce**a*|*t*| for some constants *C*, *a* > 0, then
${\displaystyle f(t)=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\widehat {f}}(\sigma +i\tau )e^{i2\pi \xi t}\,d\sigma ,}$${\displaystyle f(t)=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\widehat {f}}(\sigma +i\tau )e^{i2\pi \xi t}\,d\sigma ,}$
for any *П„* < вҲ’вҒ *a*/2ПҖвҒ .

This theorem implies the [Mellin inversion formula](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inverse_Laplace_transform#Mellin's_inverse_formula "Inverse Laplace transform") for the Laplace transformation,
${\displaystyle f(t)={\frac {1}{i2\pi }}\int \_{b-i\infty }^{b+i\infty }F(s)e^{st}\,ds}$${\displaystyle f(t)={\frac {1}{i2\pi }}\int \_{b-i\infty }^{b+i\infty }F(s)e^{st}\,ds}$
for any *b* > *a*, where *F*(*s*) is the Laplace transform of *f*(*t*).

The hypotheses can be weakened, as in the results of Carleson and Hunt, to *f*(*t*) *e*вҲ’*at* being *L*1, provided that f be of bounded variation in a closed neighborhood of t (cf. [Dini test](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dini_test "Dini test")), the value of f at t be taken to be the [arithmetic mean](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arithmetic_mean "Arithmetic mean") of the left and right limits, and that the integrals be taken in the sense of Cauchy principal values.

*L*2 versions of these inversion formulas are also available.

## Fourier transform on Euclidean space

The Fourier transform can be defined in any arbitrary number of dimensions n. As with the one-dimensional case, there are many conventions. For an integrable function *f*(**x**), this article takes the definition:
${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}({\boldsymbol {\xi }})={\mathcal {F}}(f)({\boldsymbol {\xi }})=\int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}f(\mathbf {x} )e^{-i2\pi {\boldsymbol {\xi }}\cdot \mathbf {x} }\,d\mathbf {x} }$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}({\boldsymbol {\xi }})={\mathcal {F}}(f)({\boldsymbol {\xi }})=\int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}f(\mathbf {x} )e^{-i2\pi {\boldsymbol {\xi }}\cdot \mathbf {x} }\,d\mathbf {x} }$
where **x** and **Оҫ** are n-dimensional [vectors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vector_(mathematics) "Vector (mathematics)"), and **x** В· **Оҫ** is the [dot product](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dot_product "Dot product") of the vectors. Alternatively, **Оҫ** can be viewed as belonging to the [dual vector space](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dual_space "Dual space") вҒ ${\displaystyle \mathbb {R} ^{n\star }}$${\displaystyle \mathbb {R} ^{n\star }}$вҒ , in which case the dot product becomes the [contraction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tensor_contraction "Tensor contraction") of **x** and **Оҫ**, usually written as вҹЁ**x**, **Оҫ**вҹ©.

All of the basic properties listed above hold for the n-dimensional Fourier transform, as do Plancherel's and Parseval's theorem. When the function is integrable, the Fourier transform is still uniformly continuous and the [RiemannвҖ“Lebesgue lemma](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Riemann%E2%80%93Lebesgue_lemma "RiemannвҖ“Lebesgue lemma") holds.

### Uncertainty principle

Generally speaking, the more concentrated *f*(*x*) is, the more spread out its Fourier transform *fМӮ*(*Оҫ*) must be. In particular, the scaling property of the Fourier transform may be seen as saying: if we squeeze a function in x, its Fourier transform stretches out in Оҫ. It is not possible to arbitrarily concentrate both a function and its Fourier transform.

The trade-off between the compaction of a function and its Fourier transform can be formalized in the form of an [uncertainty principle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uncertainty_principle "Uncertainty principle") by viewing a function and its Fourier transform as [conjugate variables](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conjugate_variables "Conjugate variables") with respect to the [symplectic form](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Symplectic_form "Symplectic form") on the [timeвҖ“frequency domain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Time%E2%80%93frequency_representation "TimeвҖ“frequency representation"): from the point of view of the [linear canonical transformation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linear_canonical_transformation "Linear canonical transformation"), the Fourier transform is rotation by 90В° in the timeвҖ“frequency domain, and preserves the [symplectic form](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Symplectic_vector_space "Symplectic vector space").

Suppose *f*(*x*) is an integrable and [square-integrable](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Square-integrable "Square-integrable") function. [Without loss of generality](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Without_loss_of_generality "Without loss of generality"), assume that *f*(*x*) is normalized:
${\displaystyle \int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }|f(x)|^{2}\,dx=1.}$${\displaystyle \int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }|f(x)|^{2}\,dx=1.}$

It follows from the [Plancherel theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plancherel_theorem "Plancherel theorem") that *fМӮ*(*Оҫ*) is also normalized.

The spread around *x* = 0 may be measured by the *dispersion about zero* defined by
${\displaystyle D\_{0}(f)=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }x^{2}|f(x)|^{2}\,dx.}$${\displaystyle D\_{0}(f)=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }x^{2}|f(x)|^{2}\,dx.}$

In probability terms, this is the [second moment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moment_(mathematics) "Moment (mathematics)") of |*f*(*x*)|2 about zero.

The uncertainty principle states that, if *f*(*x*) is absolutely continuous and the functions *x*В·*f*(*x*) and *f*вҖІ(*x*) are square integrable, then
${\displaystyle D\_{0}(f)D\_{0}({\widehat {f}})\geq {\frac {1}{16\pi ^{2}}}.}$${\displaystyle D\_{0}(f)D\_{0}({\widehat {f}})\geq {\frac {1}{16\pi ^{2}}}.}$

The equality is attained only in the case
${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}f(x)&=C\_{1}\,e^{-\pi {\frac {x^{2}}{\sigma ^{2}}}}\\\therefore {\widehat {f}}(\xi )&=\sigma C\_{1}\,e^{-\pi \sigma ^{2}\xi ^{2}}\end{aligned}}}$${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}f(x)&=C\_{1}\,e^{-\pi {\frac {x^{2}}{\sigma ^{2}}}}\\\therefore {\widehat {f}}(\xi )&=\sigma C\_{1}\,e^{-\pi \sigma ^{2}\xi ^{2}}\end{aligned}}}$
where *Пғ* > 0 is arbitrary and *C*1 = вҒ 4вҲҡ2/вҲҡ*Пғ*вҒ  so that f is *L*2-normalized. In other words, where f is a (normalized) [Gaussian function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gaussian_function "Gaussian function") with variance *Пғ*2/2ПҖ, centered at zero, and its Fourier transform is a Gaussian function with variance *Пғ*вҲ’2/2ПҖ. Gaussian functions are examples of [Schwartz functions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Schwartz_function "Schwartz function") (see the discussion on tempered distributions below).

In fact, this inequality implies that:
${\displaystyle \left(\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }(x-x\_{0})^{2}|f(x)|^{2}\,dx\right)\left(\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }(\xi -\xi \_{0})^{2}\left|{\widehat {f}}(\xi )\right|^{2}\,d\xi \right)\geq {\frac {1}{16\pi ^{2}}},\quad \forall x\_{0},\xi \_{0}\in \mathbb {R} .}$${\displaystyle \left(\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }(x-x\_{0})^{2}|f(x)|^{2}\,dx\right)\left(\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }(\xi -\xi \_{0})^{2}\left|{\widehat {f}}(\xi )\right|^{2}\,d\xi \right)\geq {\frac {1}{16\pi ^{2}}},\quad \forall x\_{0},\xi \_{0}\in \mathbb {R} .}$
In [quantum mechanics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_mechanics "Quantum mechanics"), the [momentum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Momentum "Momentum") and position [wave functions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wave_function "Wave function") are Fourier transform pairs, up to a factor of the [Planck constant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Planck_constant "Planck constant"). With this constant properly taken into account, the inequality above becomes the statement of the [Heisenberg uncertainty principle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heisenberg_uncertainty_principle "Heisenberg uncertainty principle").

A stronger uncertainty principle is the [Hirschman uncertainty principle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hirschman_uncertainty "Hirschman uncertainty"), which is expressed as:
${\displaystyle H\left(\left|f\right|^{2}\right)+H\left(\left|{\widehat {f}}\right|^{2}\right)\geq \log \left({\frac {e}{2}}\right)}$${\displaystyle H\left(\left|f\right|^{2}\right)+H\left(\left|{\widehat {f}}\right|^{2}\right)\geq \log \left({\frac {e}{2}}\right)}$
where *H*(*p*) is the [differential entropy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Differential_entropy "Differential entropy") of the [probability density function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Probability_density_function "Probability density function") *p*(*x*):
${\displaystyle H(p)=-\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }p(x)\log {\bigl (}p(x){\bigr )}\,dx}$${\displaystyle H(p)=-\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }p(x)\log {\bigl (}p(x){\bigr )}\,dx}$
where the logarithms may be in any base that is consistent. The equality is attained for a Gaussian, as in the previous case.

### Sine and cosine transforms

Fourier's original formulation of the transform did not use complex numbers, but rather sines and cosines. Statisticians and others still use this form. An absolutely integrable function f for which Fourier inversion holds can be expanded in terms of genuine frequencies (avoiding negative frequencies, which are sometimes considered hard to interpret physically) О» by
${\displaystyle f(t)=\int \_{0}^{\infty }{\bigl (}a(\lambda )\cos(2\pi \lambda t)+b(\lambda )\sin(2\pi \lambda t){\bigr )}\,d\lambda .}$${\displaystyle f(t)=\int \_{0}^{\infty }{\bigl (}a(\lambda )\cos(2\pi \lambda t)+b(\lambda )\sin(2\pi \lambda t){\bigr )}\,d\lambda .}$

This is called an expansion as a [trigonometric integral](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trigonometric_integral "Trigonometric integral"), or a Fourier integral expansion. The coefficient functions a and b can be found by using variants of the Fourier cosine transform and the Fourier sine transform (the normalisations are, again, not standardised):
${\displaystyle a(\lambda )=2\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(t)\cos(2\pi \lambda t)\,dt}$${\displaystyle a(\lambda )=2\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(t)\cos(2\pi \lambda t)\,dt}$
and
${\displaystyle b(\lambda )=2\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(t)\sin(2\pi \lambda t)\,dt.}$${\displaystyle b(\lambda )=2\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(t)\sin(2\pi \lambda t)\,dt.}$

Older literature refers to the two transform functions, the Fourier cosine transform, a, and the Fourier sine transform, b.

The function f can be recovered from the sine and cosine transform using
${\displaystyle f(t)=2\int \_{0}^{\infty }\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(\tau )\cos {\bigl (}2\pi \lambda (\tau -t){\bigr )}\,d\tau \,d\lambda .}$${\displaystyle f(t)=2\int \_{0}^{\infty }\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(\tau )\cos {\bigl (}2\pi \lambda (\tau -t){\bigr )}\,d\tau \,d\lambda .}$
together with trigonometric identities. This is referred to as Fourier's integral formula.

### Spherical harmonics

Let the set of [homogeneous](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homogeneous_polynomial "Homogeneous polynomial") [harmonic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harmonic_function "Harmonic function") [polynomials](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polynomial "Polynomial") of degree k on **R***n* be denoted by **A***k*. The set **A***k* consists of the [solid spherical harmonics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solid_spherical_harmonics "Solid spherical harmonics") of degree k. The solid spherical harmonics play a similar role in higher dimensions to the Hermite polynomials in dimension one. Specifically, if *f*(*x*) = *e*вҲ’ПҖ|*x*|2*P*(*x*) for some *P*(*x*) in **A***k*, then вҒ ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )=i^{-k}f(\xi )}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )=i^{-k}f(\xi )}$вҒ . Let the set **H***k* be the closure in *L*2(**R***n*) of linear combinations of functions of the form *f*(|*x*|)*P*(*x*) where *P*(*x*) is in **A***k*. The space *L*2(**R***n*) is then a direct sum of the spaces **H***k* and the Fourier transform maps each space **H***k* to itself and it is possible to characterize the action of the Fourier transform on each space **H***k*.

Let *f*(*x*) = *f*0(|*x*|)*P*(*x*) (with *P*(*x*) in **A***k*), then
${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )=F\_{0}(|\xi |)P(\xi )}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )=F\_{0}(|\xi |)P(\xi )}$
where
${\displaystyle F\_{0}(r)=2\pi i^{-k}r^{-{\frac {n+2k-2}{2}}}\int \_{0}^{\infty }f\_{0}(s)J\_{\frac {n+2k-2}{2}}(2\pi rs)s^{\frac {n+2k}{2}}\,ds.}$${\displaystyle F\_{0}(r)=2\pi i^{-k}r^{-{\frac {n+2k-2}{2}}}\int \_{0}^{\infty }f\_{0}(s)J\_{\frac {n+2k-2}{2}}(2\pi rs)s^{\frac {n+2k}{2}}\,ds.}$

Here *J*(*n* + 2*k* вҲ’ 2)/2 denotes the [Bessel function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bessel_function "Bessel function") of the first kind with order вҒ *n* + 2*k* вҲ’ 2/2вҒ . When *k* = 0 this gives a useful formula for the Fourier transform of a radial function. This is essentially the [Hankel transform](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hankel_transform "Hankel transform"). Moreover, there is a simple recursion relating the cases *n* + 2 and n allowing to compute, e.g., the three-dimensional Fourier transform of a radial function from the one-dimensional one.

### Restriction problems

In higher dimensions it becomes interesting to study *restriction problems* for the Fourier transform. The Fourier transform of an integrable function is continuous and the restriction of this function to any set is defined. But for a square-integrable function the Fourier transform could be a general *class* of square integrable functions. As such, the restriction of the Fourier transform of an *L*2(**R***n*) function cannot be defined on sets of measure 0. It is still an active area of study to understand restriction problems in *L**p* for 1 < *p* < 2. It is possible in some cases to define the restriction of a Fourier transform to a set S, provided S has non-zero curvature. The case when S is the unit sphere in **R***n* is of particular interest. In this case the TomasвҖ“[Stein](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Elias_Stein "Elias Stein") restriction theorem states that the restriction of the Fourier transform to the unit sphere in **R***n* is a bounded operator on *L**p* provided 1 вүӨ *p* вүӨ вҒ 2*n* + 2/*n* + 3вҒ .

One notable difference between the Fourier transform in 1 dimension versus higher dimensions concerns the partial sum operator. Consider an increasing collection of measurable sets *E**R* indexed by *R* вҲҲ (0, вҲһ): such as balls of radius R centered at the origin, or cubes of side 2*R*. For a given integrable function f, consider the function fR defined by:
${\displaystyle f\_{R}(x)=\int \_{E\_{R}}{\widehat {f}}(\xi )e^{i2\pi x\cdot \xi }\,d\xi ,\quad x\in \mathbb {R} ^{n}.}$${\displaystyle f\_{R}(x)=\int \_{E\_{R}}{\widehat {f}}(\xi )e^{i2\pi x\cdot \xi }\,d\xi ,\quad x\in \mathbb {R} ^{n}.}$

Suppose in addition that *f* вҲҲ *L**p*(**R***n*). For *n* = 1 and 1 < *p* < вҲһ, if one takes *ER* = (вҲ’*R*, *R*), then fR converges to f in *L**p* as R tends to infinity, by the boundedness of the [Hilbert transform](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hilbert_transform "Hilbert transform"). Naively one may hope the same holds true for *n* > 1. In the case that ER is taken to be a cube with side length R, then convergence still holds. Another natural candidate is the Euclidean ball *E**R* = {*Оҫ*В : |*Оҫ*| < *R*}. In order for this partial sum operator to converge, it is necessary that the [multiplier](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multiplier_(Fourier_analysis) "Multiplier (Fourier analysis)") for the unit ball be bounded in *L**p*(**R***n*). For *n* вүҘ 2 it is a celebrated theorem of [Charles Fefferman](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charles_Fefferman "Charles Fefferman") that the multiplier for the unit ball is never bounded unless *p* = 2. In fact, when *p* вү  2, this shows that not only may fR fail to converge to f in *L**p*, but for some functions *f* вҲҲ *L**p*(**R***n*), fR is not even an element of *L**p*.

## Fourier transform on function spaces

The definition of the Fourier transform naturally extends from ${\displaystyle L^{1}(\mathbb {R} )}$${\displaystyle L^{1}(\mathbb {R} )}$ to вҒ ${\displaystyle L^{1}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$${\displaystyle L^{1}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$вҒ . That is, if ${\displaystyle f\in L^{1}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$${\displaystyle f\in L^{1}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$ then the Fourier transform
${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}:L^{1}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})\to L^{\infty }(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}:L^{1}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})\to L^{\infty }(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$ is given by ${\displaystyle f(x)\mapsto {\widehat {f}}(\xi )=\int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}f(x)e^{-i2\pi \xi \cdot x}\,dx,\quad \forall \xi \in \mathbb {R} ^{n}.}$${\displaystyle f(x)\mapsto {\widehat {f}}(\xi )=\int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}f(x)e^{-i2\pi \xi \cdot x}\,dx,\quad \forall \xi \in \mathbb {R} ^{n}.}$
This operator is [bounded](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bounded_operator "Bounded operator") as
${\displaystyle \sup \_{\xi \in \mathbb {R} ^{n}}\left\vert {\widehat {f}}(\xi )\right\vert \leq \int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}\vert f(x)\vert \,dx,}$${\displaystyle \sup \_{\xi \in \mathbb {R} ^{n}}\left\vert {\widehat {f}}(\xi )\right\vert \leq \int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}\vert f(x)\vert \,dx,}$
which shows that its [operator norm](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Operator_norm "Operator norm") is bounded by 1. The [RiemannвҖ“Lebesgue lemma](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Riemann%E2%80%93Lebesgue_lemma "RiemannвҖ“Lebesgue lemma") shows that if ${\displaystyle f\in L^{1}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$${\displaystyle f\in L^{1}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$ then its Fourier transform actually belongs to the [space of continuous functions that vanish at infinity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Function_space#Functional_analysis "Function space"), i.e., вҒ ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\in C\_{0}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})\subset L^{\infty }(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\in C\_{0}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})\subset L^{\infty }(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$вҒ . Furthermore, the [image](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Image_(mathematics) "Image (mathematics)") of ${\displaystyle L^{1}}$${\displaystyle L^{1}}$ under ${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}}$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}}$ is a strict subset of вҒ ${\displaystyle C\_{0}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$${\displaystyle C\_{0}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$вҒ .

Similarly to the case of one variable, the Fourier transform can be defined on вҒ ${\displaystyle L^{2}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$${\displaystyle L^{2}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$вҒ . The Fourier transform in ${\displaystyle L^{2}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$${\displaystyle L^{2}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$ is no longer given by an ordinary Lebesgue integral, although it can be computed by an [improper integral](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Improper_integral "Improper integral"), i.e.,
${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )=\lim \_{R\to \infty }\int \_{|x|\leq R}f(x)e^{-i2\pi \xi \cdot x}\,dx}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )=\lim \_{R\to \infty }\int \_{|x|\leq R}f(x)e^{-i2\pi \xi \cdot x}\,dx}$
where the limit is taken in the *L*2 sense.

Furthermore, ${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}:L^{2}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})\to L^{2}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}:L^{2}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})\to L^{2}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$ is a [unitary operator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unitary_operator "Unitary operator"). For an operator to be unitary it is sufficient to show that it is bijective and preserves the inner product, so in this case these follow from the Fourier inversion theorem combined with the fact that for any *f*, *g* вҲҲ *L*2(**R***n*) we have
${\displaystyle \int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}f(x){\mathcal {F}}g(x)\,dx=\int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}{\mathcal {F}}f(x)g(x)\,dx.}$${\displaystyle \int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}f(x){\mathcal {F}}g(x)\,dx=\int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}{\mathcal {F}}f(x)g(x)\,dx.}$

In particular, the image of *L*2(**R***n*) is itself under the Fourier transform.

### On other *L**p*

For вҒ ${\displaystyle 1<p<2}$${\displaystyle 1<p<2}$вҒ , the Fourier transform can be defined on ${\displaystyle L^{p}(\mathbb {R} )}$${\displaystyle L^{p}(\mathbb {R} )}$ by [Marcinkiewicz interpolation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marcinkiewicz_interpolation "Marcinkiewicz interpolation"), which amounts to decomposing such functions into a fat tail part in *L*2 plus a fat body part in *L*1. In each of these spaces, the Fourier transform of a function in *L**p*(**R***n*) is in *L**q*(**R***n*), where *q* = вҒ *p*/*p* вҲ’ 1вҒ  is the [HГ¶lder conjugate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/H%C3%B6lder_conjugate "HГ¶lder conjugate") of p (by the [HausdorffвҖ“Young inequality](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hausdorff%E2%80%93Young_inequality "HausdorffвҖ“Young inequality")). However, except for *p* = 2, the image is not easily characterized. Further extensions become more technical. The Fourier transform of functions in *L**p* for the range 2 < *p* < вҲһ requires the study of distributions. In fact, it can be shown that there are functions in *L**p* with *p* > 2 so that the Fourier transform is not defined as a function.

### Tempered distributions

One might consider enlarging the domain of the Fourier transform from ${\displaystyle L^{1}+L^{2}}$${\displaystyle L^{1}+L^{2}}$ by considering [generalized functions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Generalized_function "Generalized function"), or distributions. A distribution on ${\displaystyle \mathbb {R} ^{n}}$${\displaystyle \mathbb {R} ^{n}}$ is a continuous linear functional on the space ${\displaystyle C\_{c}^{\infty }(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$${\displaystyle C\_{c}^{\infty }(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$ of compactly supported smooth functions (i.e. [bump functions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bump_function "Bump function")), equipped with a suitable topology. Since ${\displaystyle C\_{c}^{\infty }(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$${\displaystyle C\_{c}^{\infty }(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$ is dense in вҒ ${\displaystyle L^{2}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$${\displaystyle L^{2}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$вҒ , the [Plancherel theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plancherel_theorem "Plancherel theorem") allows one to extend the definition of the Fourier transform to general functions in ${\displaystyle L^{2}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$${\displaystyle L^{2}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$ by continuity arguments. The strategy is then to consider the action of the Fourier transform on ${\displaystyle C\_{c}^{\infty }(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$${\displaystyle C\_{c}^{\infty }(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$ and pass to distributions by duality. The obstruction to doing this is that the Fourier transform does not map ${\displaystyle C\_{c}^{\infty }(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$${\displaystyle C\_{c}^{\infty }(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$ to вҒ ${\displaystyle C\_{c}^{\infty }(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$${\displaystyle C\_{c}^{\infty }(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$вҒ . In fact the Fourier transform of an element in ${\displaystyle C\_{c}^{\infty }(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$${\displaystyle C\_{c}^{\infty }(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$ can not vanish on an open set; see the above discussion on the uncertainty principle.

The Fourier transform can also be defined for [tempered distributions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tempered_distribution "Tempered distribution") вҒ ${\displaystyle {\mathcal {S}}'(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {S}}'(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$вҒ , dual to the space of [Schwartz functions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Schwartz_function "Schwartz function") вҒ ${\displaystyle {\mathcal {S}}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {S}}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$вҒ . A Schwartz function is a smooth function that decays at infinity, along with all of its derivatives, hence ${\displaystyle C\_{c}^{\infty }(\mathbb {R} ^{n})\subset {\mathcal {S}}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$${\displaystyle C\_{c}^{\infty }(\mathbb {R} ^{n})\subset {\mathcal {S}}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})}$ and:
${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}:C\_{c}^{\infty }(\mathbb {R} ^{n})\rightarrow {\mathcal {S}}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})\setminus C\_{c}^{\infty }(\mathbb {R} ^{n}).}$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}:C\_{c}^{\infty }(\mathbb {R} ^{n})\rightarrow {\mathcal {S}}(\mathbb {R} ^{n})\setminus C\_{c}^{\infty }(\mathbb {R} ^{n}).}$ The Fourier transform is an [automorphism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Automorphism "Automorphism") of the Schwartz space and, by duality, also an automorphism of the space of tempered distributions. The tempered distributions include well-behaved functions of polynomial growth, distributions of compact support as well as all the integrable functions mentioned above.

For the definition of the Fourier transform of a tempered distribution, let ${\displaystyle f}$${\displaystyle f}$ and ${\displaystyle g}$${\displaystyle g}$ be integrable functions, and let ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$ and ${\displaystyle {\widehat {g}}}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {g}}}$ be their Fourier transforms respectively. Then the Fourier transform obeys the following multiplication formula,
${\displaystyle \int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}{\widehat {f}}(x)g(x)\,dx=\int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}f(x){\widehat {g}}(x)\,dx.}$${\displaystyle \int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}{\widehat {f}}(x)g(x)\,dx=\int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}f(x){\widehat {g}}(x)\,dx.}$

Every integrable function ${\displaystyle f}$${\displaystyle f}$ defines (induces) a distribution ${\displaystyle T\_{f}}$${\displaystyle T\_{f}}$ by the relation
${\displaystyle T\_{f}(\varphi )=\int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}f(x)\varphi (x)\,dx,\quad \forall \varphi \in {\mathcal {S}}(\mathbb {R} ^{n}).}$${\displaystyle T\_{f}(\varphi )=\int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}f(x)\varphi (x)\,dx,\quad \forall \varphi \in {\mathcal {S}}(\mathbb {R} ^{n}).}$
So it makes sense to define the Fourier transform of a tempered distribution ${\displaystyle T\_{f}\in {\mathcal {S}}'(\mathbb {R} )}$${\displaystyle T\_{f}\in {\mathcal {S}}'(\mathbb {R} )}$ by the duality:
${\displaystyle \langle {\widehat {T}}\_{f},\varphi \rangle =\langle T\_{f},{\widehat {\varphi }}\rangle ,\quad \forall \varphi \in {\mathcal {S}}(\mathbb {R} ^{n}).}$${\displaystyle \langle {\widehat {T}}\_{f},\varphi \rangle =\langle T\_{f},{\widehat {\varphi }}\rangle ,\quad \forall \varphi \in {\mathcal {S}}(\mathbb {R} ^{n}).}$
Extending this to all tempered distributions ${\displaystyle T}$${\displaystyle T}$ gives the general definition of the Fourier transform.

Distributions can be differentiated and the above-mentioned compatibility of the Fourier transform with differentiation and convolution remains true for tempered distributions.

## Generalizations

### FourierвҖ“Stieltjes transform on measurable spaces

The Fourier transform of a [finite](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Finite_measure "Finite measure") [Borel measure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Borel_measure "Borel measure") Ој on **R***n*, given by the bounded, uniformly continuous function:
${\displaystyle {\widehat {\mu }}(\xi )=\int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}e^{-i2\pi x\cdot \xi }\,d\mu ,}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {\mu }}(\xi )=\int \_{\mathbb {R} ^{n}}e^{-i2\pi x\cdot \xi }\,d\mu ,}$
is called the *FourierвҖ“Stieltjes transform* due to its connection with the [Riemann-Stieltjes integral](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Riemann%E2%80%93Stieltjes_integral#Application_to_functional_analysis "RiemannвҖ“Stieltjes integral") representation of [(Radon) measures](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radon_measure "Radon measure"). If ${\displaystyle \mu }$${\displaystyle \mu }$ is the [probability distribution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Probability_distribution "Probability distribution") of a [random variable](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Random_variable "Random variable") ${\displaystyle X}$${\displaystyle X}$ then its FourierвҖ“Stieltjes transform is, by definition, a [characteristic function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Characteristic_function_(probability_theory) "Characteristic function (probability theory)"). If, in addition, the probability distribution has a [probability density function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Probability_density_function "Probability density function"), this definition is subject to the usual Fourier transform. Stated more generally, when ${\displaystyle \mu }$${\displaystyle \mu }$ is [absolutely continuous](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Absolute_continuity#Absolute_continuity_of_measures "Absolute continuity") with respect to the Lebesgue measure, i.e.,
${\displaystyle d\mu =f(x)\,dx,}$${\displaystyle d\mu =f(x)\,dx,}$
then
${\displaystyle {\widehat {\mu }}(\xi )={\widehat {f}}(\xi ),}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {\mu }}(\xi )={\widehat {f}}(\xi ),}$
and the Fourier-Stieltjes transform reduces to the usual definition of the Fourier transform. That is, the notable difference with the Fourier transform of integrable functions is that the Fourier-Stieltjes transform need not vanish at infinity, i.e., the [RiemannвҖ“Lebesgue lemma](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Riemann%E2%80%93Lebesgue_lemma "RiemannвҖ“Lebesgue lemma") fails for measures.

[Bochner's theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bochner%27s_theorem "Bochner's theorem") characterizes which functions may arise as the FourierвҖ“Stieltjes transform of a positive measure on the circle.

One example of a finite Borel measure that is not a function is the [Dirac measure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dirac_measure "Dirac measure"). Its Fourier transform is a constant function (whose value depends on the form of the Fourier transform used).

### Locally compact abelian groups

The Fourier transform may be generalized to any [locally compact abelian group](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Locally_compact_abelian_group "Locally compact abelian group"), i.e., an [abelian group](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abelian_group "Abelian group") that is also a [locally compact Hausdorff space](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Locally_compact_Hausdorff_space "Locally compact Hausdorff space") such that the group operation is continuous. If G is a locally compact abelian group, it has a translation invariant measure Ој, called [Haar measure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Haar_measure "Haar measure"). For a locally compact abelian group G, the set of irreducible, i.e. one-dimensional, unitary representations are called its [characters](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Character_group "Character group"). With its natural group structure and the topology of uniform convergence on compact sets (that is, the topology induced by the [compact-open topology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Compact-open_topology "Compact-open topology") on the space of all continuous functions from ${\displaystyle G}$${\displaystyle G}$ to the [circle group](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Circle_group "Circle group")), the set of characters Дң is itself a locally compact abelian group, called the *Pontryagin dual* of G. For a function f in *L*1(*G*), its Fourier transform is defined by
${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )=\int \_{G}{\overline {\xi (x)}}f(x)\,d\mu \quad {\text{for any }}\xi \in {\widehat {G}}.}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )=\int \_{G}{\overline {\xi (x)}}f(x)\,d\mu \quad {\text{for any }}\xi \in {\widehat {G}}.}$

The RiemannвҖ“Lebesgue lemma holds in this case; *fМӮ*(*Оҫ*) is a function vanishing at infinity on Дң.

The Fourier transform on T = R/Z is an example; here T is a locally compact abelian group, and the Haar measure Ој on T can be thought of as the Lebesgue measure on [0,1). Consider a representation of T on the complex plane C thought of as a 1-dimensional complex vector space. There is a group of such representations (which are irreducible since C is 1-dim) ${\displaystyle \{e\_{k}:T\rightarrow GL\_{1}(C)=C^{\*}\mid k\in Z\}}$${\displaystyle \{e\_{k}:T\rightarrow GL\_{1}(C)=C^{\*}\mid k\in Z\}}$ where ${\displaystyle e\_{k}(x)=e^{i2\pi kx}}$${\displaystyle e\_{k}(x)=e^{i2\pi kx}}$ for вҒ ${\displaystyle x\in T}$${\displaystyle x\in T}$вҒ .

The character of such representation, that is the trace of ${\displaystyle e\_{k}(x)}$${\displaystyle e\_{k}(x)}$ (thought of as a one-by-one matrix) for each ${\displaystyle x\in T}$${\displaystyle x\in T}$ and вҒ ${\displaystyle k\in Z}$${\displaystyle k\in Z}$вҒ , is ${\displaystyle e^{i2\pi kx}}$${\displaystyle e^{i2\pi kx}}$ itself. Now, in the case of representations of finite groups, the character table of a group G consists of rows of vectors such that each row is the character of one irreducible representation of G, and these vectors form an orthonormal basis of the space of class (meaning conjugation-invariant) functions that map from G to C by Schur's lemma. The group T is no longer finite but still compact, and it preserves the orthonormality of the character table. Each row of the table is the function ${\displaystyle e\_{k}(x)}$${\displaystyle e\_{k}(x)}$ of вҒ ${\displaystyle x\in T}$${\displaystyle x\in T}$вҒ , and the inner product between two class functions (all functions being class functions since T is abelian) ${\displaystyle f,g\in L^{2}(T,d\mu )}$${\displaystyle f,g\in L^{2}(T,d\mu )}$ is defined as ${\textstyle \langle f,g\rangle ={\frac {1}{|T|}}\int \_{[0,1)}f(y){\overline {g}}(y)d\mu (y)}$${\textstyle \langle f,g\rangle ={\frac {1}{|T|}}\int \_{[0,1)}f(y){\overline {g}}(y)d\mu (y)}$ with the normalizing factor вҒ ${\displaystyle \vert T\vert =1}$${\displaystyle \vert T\vert =1}$вҒ . The sequence ${\displaystyle \{e\_{k}\mid k\in Z\}}$${\displaystyle \{e\_{k}\mid k\in Z\}}$ is an orthonormal basis of the space of class functions вҒ ${\displaystyle L^{2}(T,d\mu )}$${\displaystyle L^{2}(T,d\mu )}$вҒ .

For any representation V of a finite group G, ${\displaystyle \chi \_{v}}$${\displaystyle \chi \_{v}}$ can be expressed as the span ${\textstyle \sum \_{i}\left\langle \chi \_{v},\chi \_{v\_{i}}\right\rangle \chi \_{v\_{i}}}$${\textstyle \sum \_{i}\left\langle \chi \_{v},\chi \_{v\_{i}}\right\rangle \chi \_{v\_{i}}}$ (${\displaystyle V\_{i}}$${\displaystyle V\_{i}}$ are the irreducible representations of G), such that вҒ ${\displaystyle \textstyle \left\langle \chi \_{v},\chi \_{v\_{i}}\right\rangle ={\frac {1}{\vert G\vert }}\sum \_{g\in G}\chi \_{v}(g){\overline {\chi }}\_{v\_{i}}(g)}$${\displaystyle \textstyle \left\langle \chi \_{v},\chi \_{v\_{i}}\right\rangle ={\frac {1}{\vert G\vert }}\sum \_{g\in G}\chi \_{v}(g){\overline {\chi }}\_{v\_{i}}(g)}$вҒ . Similarly for ${\displaystyle G=T}$${\displaystyle G=T}$ and вҒ ${\displaystyle f\in L^{2}(T,d\mu )}$${\displaystyle f\in L^{2}(T,d\mu )}$вҒ , вҒ ${\displaystyle \textstyle f(x)=\sum \_{k\in Z}{\widehat {f}}(k)e\_{k}}$${\displaystyle \textstyle f(x)=\sum \_{k\in Z}{\widehat {f}}(k)e\_{k}}$вҒ . The Pontriagin dual ${\displaystyle {\widehat {T}}}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {T}}}$ is ${\displaystyle \{e\_{k}\}(k\in Z)}$${\displaystyle \{e\_{k}\}(k\in Z)}$ and for вҒ ${\displaystyle f\in L^{2}(T,d\mu )}$${\displaystyle f\in L^{2}(T,d\mu )}$вҒ , ${\textstyle {\widehat {f}}(k)={\frac {1}{|T|}}\int \_{[0,1)}f(y)e^{-i2\pi ky}dy}$${\textstyle {\widehat {f}}(k)={\frac {1}{|T|}}\int \_{[0,1)}f(y)e^{-i2\pi ky}dy}$ is its Fourier transform for вҒ ${\displaystyle e\_{k}\in {\widehat {T}}}$${\displaystyle e\_{k}\in {\widehat {T}}}$вҒ .

### Gelfand transform

The Fourier transform is also a special case of the [Gelfand transform](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gelfand_transform "Gelfand transform"). In this particular context, it is closely related to the Pontryagin duality map defined above.

Given an abelian [locally compact](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Locally_compact_space "Locally compact space") [Hausdorff](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hausdorff_space "Hausdorff space") [topological group](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Topological_group "Topological group") G, as before we consider the space *L*1(*G*), defined using a Haar measure. With convolution as multiplication, *L*1(*G*) is an abelian [Banach algebra](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Banach_algebra "Banach algebra"). It also has an [involution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Involution_(mathematics) "Involution (mathematics)") \* given by
${\displaystyle f^{\*}(g)={\overline {f\left(g^{-1}\right)}}.}$${\displaystyle f^{\*}(g)={\overline {f\left(g^{-1}\right)}}.}$

Taking the completion with respect to the largest possible *C*\*-norm gives its enveloping *C*\*-algebra, called the group *C*\*-algebra *C*\*(*G*) of G. (Any *C*\*-norm on *L*1(*G*) is bounded by the *L*1 norm, therefore their supremum exists.)

Given any abelian *C*\*-algebra A, the Gelfand transform gives an isomorphism between A and *C*0(*A*^), where *A*^ is the multiplicative linear functionals, i.e. one-dimensional representations, on A with the weak-\* topology. The map is simply given by
${\displaystyle a\mapsto {\bigl (}\varphi \mapsto \varphi (a){\bigr )}.}$${\displaystyle a\mapsto {\bigl (}\varphi \mapsto \varphi (a){\bigr )}.}$
It turns out that the multiplicative linear functionals of *C*\*(*G*), after suitable identification, are exactly the characters of G, and the Gelfand transform, when restricted to the dense subset *L*1(*G*), is the FourierвҖ“Pontryagin transform.

### Compact non-abelian groups

The Fourier transform can also be defined for functions on a non-abelian group, provided that the group is [compact](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Compact_space "Compact space"). Removing the assumption that the underlying group is abelian, irreducible unitary representations need not always be one-dimensional. This means the Fourier transform on a non-abelian group takes values as Hilbert space operators. The Fourier transform on compact groups is a major tool in [representation theory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Representation_theory "Representation theory") and [non-commutative harmonic analysis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-commutative_harmonic_analysis "Non-commutative harmonic analysis").

Let G be a compact [Hausdorff](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hausdorff_space "Hausdorff space") [topological group](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Topological_group "Topological group"). Let ОЈ denote the collection of all isomorphism classes of finite-dimensional irreducible [unitary representations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unitary_representation "Unitary representation"), along with a definite choice of representation *U*(*Пғ*) on the [Hilbert space](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hilbert_space "Hilbert space") *HПғ* of finite dimension *dПғ* for each *Пғ* вҲҲ ОЈ. If Ој is a finite [Borel measure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Borel_measure "Borel measure") on G, then the FourierвҖ“Stieltjes transform of Ој is the operator on *HПғ* defined by
${\displaystyle \left\langle {\widehat {\mu }}\xi ,\eta \right\rangle \_{H\_{\sigma }}=\int \_{G}\left\langle {\overline {U}}\_{g}^{(\sigma )}\xi ,\eta \right\rangle \,d\mu (g)}$${\displaystyle \left\langle {\widehat {\mu }}\xi ,\eta \right\rangle \_{H\_{\sigma }}=\int \_{G}\left\langle {\overline {U}}\_{g}^{(\sigma )}\xi ,\eta \right\rangle \,d\mu (g)}$
where *U*(*Пғ*) is the complex-conjugate representation of *U*(*Пғ*) acting on *HПғ*. If Ој is [absolutely continuous](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Absolutely_continuous "Absolutely continuous") with respect to the [left-invariant probability measure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Haar_measure "Haar measure") О» on G, [represented](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radon%E2%80%93Nikodym_theorem "RadonвҖ“Nikodym theorem") as
${\displaystyle d\mu =f\,d\lambda }$${\displaystyle d\mu =f\,d\lambda }$
for some *f* вҲҲ [*L*1(*О»*)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lp_space "Lp space"), one identifies the Fourier transform of f with the FourierвҖ“Stieltjes transform of Ој.

The mapping
${\displaystyle \mu \mapsto {\widehat {\mu }}}$${\displaystyle \mu \mapsto {\widehat {\mu }}}$
defines an isomorphism between the [Banach space](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Banach_space "Banach space") *M*(*G*) of finite Borel measures (see *[Rca space](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rca_space "Rca space")*) and a closed subspace of the Banach space **C**вҲһ(ОЈ) consisting of all sequences *E* = (*EПғ*) indexed by ОЈ of (bounded) linear operators *EПғ*В : *HПғ* вҶ’ *HПғ* for which the norm
${\displaystyle \|E\|=\sup \_{\sigma \in \Sigma }\left\|E\_{\sigma }\right\|}$${\displaystyle \|E\|=\sup \_{\sigma \in \Sigma }\left\|E\_{\sigma }\right\|}$
is finite. The "[convolution theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Convolution_theorem "Convolution theorem")" asserts that, furthermore, this isomorphism of Banach spaces is in fact an isometric isomorphism of [C\*-algebras](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/C*-algebra "C*-algebra") into a subspace of **C**вҲһ(ОЈ). Multiplication on *M*(*G*) is given by [convolution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Convolution "Convolution") of measures and the involution \* defined by
${\displaystyle f^{\*}(g)={\overline {f\left(g^{-1}\right)}},}$${\displaystyle f^{\*}(g)={\overline {f\left(g^{-1}\right)}},}$
and **C**вҲһ(ОЈ) has a natural *C*\*-algebra structure as Hilbert space operators.

The [PeterвҖ“Weyl theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peter%E2%80%93Weyl_theorem "PeterвҖ“Weyl theorem") holds, and a version of the Fourier inversion formula ([Plancherel's theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plancherel%27s_theorem "Plancherel's theorem")) follows: if *f* вҲҲ *L*2(*G*), then
${\displaystyle f(g)=\sum \_{\sigma \in \Sigma }d\_{\sigma }\operatorname {tr} \left({\widehat {f}}(\sigma )U\_{g}^{(\sigma )}\right)}$${\displaystyle f(g)=\sum \_{\sigma \in \Sigma }d\_{\sigma }\operatorname {tr} \left({\widehat {f}}(\sigma )U\_{g}^{(\sigma )}\right)}$
where the summation is understood as convergent in the *L*2 sense.

The generalization of the Fourier transform to the noncommutative situation has also in part contributed to the development of [noncommutative geometry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Noncommutative_geometry "Noncommutative geometry").[*[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In this context, a categorical generalization of the Fourier transform to noncommutative groups is [TannakaвҖ“Krein duality](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tannaka%E2%80%93Krein_duality "TannakaвҖ“Krein duality"), which replaces the group of characters with the category of representations. However, this loses the connection with harmonic functions.

## Alternatives

In [signal processing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Signal_processing "Signal processing") terms, a function (of time) is a representation of a signal with perfect *time resolution*, but no frequency information, while the Fourier transform has perfect *frequency resolution*, but no time information: the magnitude of the Fourier transform at a point is how much frequency content there is, but location is only given by phase (argument of the Fourier transform at a point), and [standing waves](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standing_wave "Standing wave") are not localized in time вҖ“ a sine wave continues out to infinity, without decaying. This limits the usefulness of the Fourier transform for analyzing signals that are localized in time, notably [transients](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transient_(acoustics) "Transient (acoustics)"), or any signal of finite extent.

As alternatives to the Fourier transform, in [timeвҖ“frequency analysis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Time%E2%80%93frequency_analysis "TimeвҖ“frequency analysis"), one uses timeвҖ“frequency transforms or timeвҖ“frequency distributions to represent signals in a form that has some time information and some frequency information вҖ“ by the uncertainty principle, there is a trade-off between these. These can be generalizations of the Fourier transform, such as the [short-time Fourier transform](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Short-time_Fourier_transform "Short-time Fourier transform"), [fractional Fourier transform](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractional_Fourier_transform "Fractional Fourier transform"), synchrosqueezing Fourier transform, or other functions to represent signals, as in [wavelet transforms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wavelet_transform "Wavelet transform") and [chirplet transforms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chirplet_transform "Chirplet transform"), with the wavelet analog of the (continuous) Fourier transform being the [continuous wavelet transform](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Continuous_wavelet_transform "Continuous wavelet transform").

## Example

The following figures provide a visual illustration of how the Fourier transform's integral measures whether a frequency is present in a particular function. The first image depicts the function вҒ ${\displaystyle f(t)=\cos(2\pi \ 3t)\ e^{-\pi t^{2}}}$${\displaystyle f(t)=\cos(2\pi \ 3t)\ e^{-\pi t^{2}}}$вҒ , which is a 3В [Hz](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hertz "Hertz") cosine wave (the first term) shaped by a [Gaussian](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gaussian_function "Gaussian function") [envelope function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Envelope_(waves) "Envelope (waves)") (the second term) that smoothly turns the wave on and off. The next 2 images show the product вҒ ${\displaystyle f(t)e^{-i2\pi 3t}}$${\displaystyle f(t)e^{-i2\pi 3t}}$вҒ , which must be integrated to calculate the Fourier transform at +3В Hz. The real part of the integrand has a non-negative average value, because the alternating signs of ${\displaystyle f(t)}$${\displaystyle f(t)}$ and ${\displaystyle \operatorname {Re} (e^{-i2\pi 3t})}$${\displaystyle \operatorname {Re} (e^{-i2\pi 3t})}$ oscillate at the same rate and in phase, whereas ${\displaystyle f(t)}$${\displaystyle f(t)}$ and ${\displaystyle \operatorname {Im} (e^{-i2\pi 3t})}$${\displaystyle \operatorname {Im} (e^{-i2\pi 3t})}$ oscillate at the same rate but with orthogonal phase. The absolute value of the Fourier transform at +3В Hz is 0.5, which is relatively large. When added to the Fourier transform at -3В Hz (which is identical because we started with a real signal), we find that the amplitude of the 3В Hz frequency component is 1.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Onfreq.png/960px-Onfreq.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Onfreq.png)

Original function, which has a strong 3В Hz component. Real and imaginary parts of the integrand of its Fourier transform at +3В Hz.

However, when you try to measure a frequency that is not present, both the real and imaginary component of the integral vary rapidly between positive and negative values. For instance, the red curve is looking for 5В Hz. The absolute value of its integral is nearly zero, indicating that almost no 5В Hz component was in the signal. The general situation is usually more complicated than this, but heuristically this is how the Fourier transform measures how much of an individual frequency is present in a function вҒ ${\displaystyle f(t)}$${\displaystyle f(t)}$вҒ .

* [![Real and imaginary parts of the integrand for its Fourier transform at +5В Hz.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Offfreq_i2p.svg/500px-Offfreq_i2p.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Offfreq_i2p.svg "Real and imaginary parts of the integrand for its Fourier transform at +5В Hz.")

  Real and imaginary parts of the integrand for its Fourier transform at +5В Hz.
* [![Magnitude of its Fourier transform, with +3 and +5В Hz labeled.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Fourier_transform_of_oscillating_function.svg/500px-Fourier_transform_of_oscillating_function.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Fourier_transform_of_oscillating_function.svg "Magnitude of its Fourier transform, with +3 and +5В Hz labeled.")

  Magnitude of its Fourier transform, with +3 and +5В Hz labeled.

To re-enforce an earlier point, the reason for the response at ${\displaystyle \xi =-3}$${\displaystyle \xi =-3}$ Hz is because ${\displaystyle \cos(2\pi 3t)}$${\displaystyle \cos(2\pi 3t)}$ and ${\displaystyle \cos(2\pi (-3)t)}$${\displaystyle \cos(2\pi (-3)t)}$ are indistinguishable. The transform of В ${\displaystyle e^{i2\pi 3t}\cdot e^{-\pi t^{2}}}$${\displaystyle e^{i2\pi 3t}\cdot e^{-\pi t^{2}}}$В  would have just one response, whose amplitude is the integral of the smooth envelope: вҒ ${\displaystyle e^{-\pi t^{2}}}$${\displaystyle e^{-\pi t^{2}}}$вҒ , whereas В ${\displaystyle \operatorname {Re} (f(t)\cdot e^{-i2\pi 3t})}$${\displaystyle \operatorname {Re} (f(t)\cdot e^{-i2\pi 3t})}$ is вҒ ${\displaystyle e^{-\pi t^{2}}(1+\cos(2\pi 6t))/2}$${\displaystyle e^{-\pi t^{2}}(1+\cos(2\pi 6t))/2}$вҒ .

## Applications

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Commutative_diagram_illustrating_problem_solving_via_the_Fourier_transform.svg/500px-Commutative_diagram_illustrating_problem_solving_via_the_Fourier_transform.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Commutative_diagram_illustrating_problem_solving_via_the_Fourier_transform.svg)

Some problems, such as certain differential equations, become easier to solve when the Fourier transform is applied. In that case the solution to the original problem is recovered using the inverse Fourier transform.

Linear operations performed in one domain (time or frequency) have corresponding operations in the other domain, which are sometimes easier to perform. The operation of [differentiation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Derivative "Derivative") in the time domain corresponds to multiplication by the frequency, so some [differential equations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Differential_equation "Differential equation") are easier to analyze in the frequency domain. Also, [convolution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Convolution "Convolution") in the time domain corresponds to ordinary multiplication in the frequency domain (see *[Convolution theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Convolution_theorem "Convolution theorem")*). After performing the desired operations, transformation of the result can be made back to the time domain. [Harmonic analysis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harmonic_analysis "Harmonic analysis") is the systematic study of the relationship between the frequency and time domains, including the kinds of functions or operations that are "simpler" in one or the other, and has deep connections to many areas of modern mathematics.

### Analysis of differential equations

Perhaps the most important use of the Fourier transformation is to solve [partial differential equations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Partial_differential_equation "Partial differential equation").
Many of the equations of the mathematical physics of the nineteenth century can be treated this way. Fourier studied the heat equation, which in one dimension and in dimensionless units is
${\displaystyle {\frac {\partial ^{2}y(x,t)}{\partial ^{2}x}}={\frac {\partial y(x,t)}{\partial t}}.}$${\displaystyle {\frac {\partial ^{2}y(x,t)}{\partial ^{2}x}}={\frac {\partial y(x,t)}{\partial t}}.}$
The example we will give, a slightly more difficult one, is the wave equation in one dimension,
${\displaystyle {\frac {\partial ^{2}y(x,t)}{\partial ^{2}x}}={\frac {\partial ^{2}y(x,t)}{\partial ^{2}t}}.}$${\displaystyle {\frac {\partial ^{2}y(x,t)}{\partial ^{2}x}}={\frac {\partial ^{2}y(x,t)}{\partial ^{2}t}}.}$

As usual, the problem is not to find a solution: there are infinitely many. The problem is that of the so-called "boundary problem": find a solution that satisfies the 'boundary conditions'
${\displaystyle y(x,0)=f(x),\qquad {\frac {\partial y(x,0)}{\partial t}}=g(x).}$${\displaystyle y(x,0)=f(x),\qquad {\frac {\partial y(x,0)}{\partial t}}=g(x).}$

Here, f and g are given functions. For the heat equation, only one boundary condition can be required (usually the first one). But for the wave equation, there are still infinitely many solutions y that satisfy the first boundary condition. But when one imposes both conditions, there is only one possible solution.

It is easier to find the Fourier transform Е· of the solution than to find the solution directly. This is because the Fourier transformation takes differentiation into multiplication by the Fourier-dual variable, and so a partial differential equation applied to the original function is transformed into multiplication by polynomial functions of the dual variables applied to the transformed function. After Е· is determined, we can apply the inverse Fourier transformation to find y.

Fourier's method is as follows. First, note that any function of the forms
${\displaystyle \cos {\bigl (}2\pi \xi (x\pm t){\bigr )}{\text{ or }}\sin {\bigl (}2\pi \xi (x\pm t){\bigr )}}$${\displaystyle \cos {\bigl (}2\pi \xi (x\pm t){\bigr )}{\text{ or }}\sin {\bigl (}2\pi \xi (x\pm t){\bigr )}}$
satisfies the wave equation. These are called the elementary solutions.

Second, note that therefore any integral
${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}y(x,t)=\int \_{0}^{\infty }d\xi {\Bigl [}&a\_{+}(\xi )\cos {\bigl (}2\pi \xi (x+t){\bigr )}+a\_{-}(\xi )\cos {\bigl (}2\pi \xi (x-t){\bigr )}+{}\\&b\_{+}(\xi )\sin {\bigl (}2\pi \xi (x+t){\bigr )}+b\_{-}(\xi )\sin \left(2\pi \xi (x-t)\right){\Bigr ]}\end{aligned}}}$${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}y(x,t)=\int \_{0}^{\infty }d\xi {\Bigl [}&a\_{+}(\xi )\cos {\bigl (}2\pi \xi (x+t){\bigr )}+a\_{-}(\xi )\cos {\bigl (}2\pi \xi (x-t){\bigr )}+{}\\&b\_{+}(\xi )\sin {\bigl (}2\pi \xi (x+t){\bigr )}+b\_{-}(\xi )\sin \left(2\pi \xi (x-t)\right){\Bigr ]}\end{aligned}}}$
satisfies the wave equation for arbitrary *a*+, *a*вҲ’, *b*+, *b*вҲ’. This integral may be interpreted as a continuous linear combination of solutions for the linear equation.

Now this resembles the formula for the Fourier synthesis of a function. In fact, this is the real inverse Fourier transform of *a*Вұ and *b*Вұ in the variable x.

The third step is to examine how to find the specific unknown coefficient functions *a*Вұ and *b*Вұ that will lead to y satisfying the boundary conditions. We are interested in the values of these solutions at *t* = 0. So we will set *t* = 0. Assuming that the conditions needed for Fourier inversion are satisfied, we can then find the Fourier sine and cosine transforms (in the variable x) of both sides and obtain
${\displaystyle 2\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }y(x,0)\cos(2\pi \xi x)\,dx=a\_{+}+a\_{-}}$${\displaystyle 2\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }y(x,0)\cos(2\pi \xi x)\,dx=a\_{+}+a\_{-}}$
and
${\displaystyle 2\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }y(x,0)\sin(2\pi \xi x)\,dx=b\_{+}+b\_{-}.}$${\displaystyle 2\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }y(x,0)\sin(2\pi \xi x)\,dx=b\_{+}+b\_{-}.}$

Similarly, taking the derivative of y with respect to t and then applying the Fourier sine and cosine transformations yields
${\displaystyle 2\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\frac {\partial y(u,0)}{\partial t}}\sin(2\pi \xi x)\,dx=(2\pi \xi )\left(-a\_{+}+a\_{-}\right)}$${\displaystyle 2\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\frac {\partial y(u,0)}{\partial t}}\sin(2\pi \xi x)\,dx=(2\pi \xi )\left(-a\_{+}+a\_{-}\right)}$
and
${\displaystyle 2\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\frac {\partial y(u,0)}{\partial t}}\cos(2\pi \xi x)\,dx=(2\pi \xi )\left(b\_{+}-b\_{-}\right).}$${\displaystyle 2\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }{\frac {\partial y(u,0)}{\partial t}}\cos(2\pi \xi x)\,dx=(2\pi \xi )\left(b\_{+}-b\_{-}\right).}$

These are four linear equations for the four unknowns *a*Вұ and *b*Вұ, in terms of the Fourier sine and cosine transforms of the boundary conditions, which are easily solved by elementary algebra, provided that these transforms can be found.

In summary, we chose a set of elementary solutions, parametrized by Оҫ, of which the general solution would be a (continuous) linear combination in the form of an integral over the parameter Оҫ. But this integral was in the form of a Fourier integral. The next step was to express the boundary conditions in terms of these integrals, and set them equal to the given functions f and g. But these expressions also took the form of a Fourier integral because of the properties of the Fourier transform of a derivative. The last step was to exploit Fourier inversion by applying the Fourier transformation to both sides, thus obtaining expressions for the coefficient functions *a*Вұ and *b*Вұ in terms of the given boundary conditions f and g.

From a higher point of view, Fourier's procedure can be reformulated more conceptually. Since there are two variables, we will use the Fourier transformation in both x and t rather than operate as Fourier did, who only transformed in the spatial variables. Note that Е· must be considered in the sense of a distribution since *y*(*x*, *t*) is not going to be *L*1: as a wave, it will persist through time and thus is not a transient phenomenon. But it will be bounded and so its Fourier transform can be defined as a distribution. The operational properties of the Fourier transformation that are relevant to this equation are that it takes differentiation in x to multiplication by *i*2ПҖ*Оҫ* and differentiation with respect to t to multiplication by *i*2ПҖ*f* where f is the frequency. Then the wave equation becomes an algebraic equation in Е·:
${\displaystyle \xi ^{2}{\widehat {y}}(\xi ,f)=f^{2}{\widehat {y}}(\xi ,f).}$${\displaystyle \xi ^{2}{\widehat {y}}(\xi ,f)=f^{2}{\widehat {y}}(\xi ,f).}$
This is equivalent to requiring *Е·*(*Оҫ*, *f*) = 0 unless *Оҫ* = Вұ*f*. Right away, this explains why the choice of elementary solutions we made earlier worked so well: obviously *Е·* = *Оҙ*(*Оҫ* Вұ *f*) will be solutions. Applying Fourier inversion to these delta functions, we obtain the elementary solutions we picked earlier. But from the higher point of view, one does not pick elementary solutions, but rather considers the space of all distributions that are supported on the (degenerate) conic *Оҫ*2 вҲ’ *f*2 = 0.

We may as well consider the distributions supported on the conic that are given by distributions of one variable on the line *Оҫ* = *f* plus distributions on the line *Оҫ* = вҲ’*f* as follows: if ОҰ is any test function,
${\displaystyle \iint {\widehat {y}}\varphi (\xi ,f)\,d\xi \,df=\int s\_{+}\varphi (\xi ,\xi )\,d\xi +\int s\_{-}\varphi (\xi ,-\xi )\,d\xi ,}$${\displaystyle \iint {\widehat {y}}\varphi (\xi ,f)\,d\xi \,df=\int s\_{+}\varphi (\xi ,\xi )\,d\xi +\int s\_{-}\varphi (\xi ,-\xi )\,d\xi ,}$
where *s*+, and *s*вҲ’, are distributions of one variable.

Then Fourier inversion gives, for the boundary conditions, something very similar to what we had more concretely above (put *ОҰ*(*Оҫ*, *f*) = *e**i*2ПҖ(*xОҫ*+*tf*), which is clearly of polynomial growth):
${\displaystyle y(x,0)=\int {\bigl \{}s\_{+}(\xi )+s\_{-}(\xi ){\bigr \}}e^{i2\pi \xi x+0}\,d\xi }$${\displaystyle y(x,0)=\int {\bigl \{}s\_{+}(\xi )+s\_{-}(\xi ){\bigr \}}e^{i2\pi \xi x+0}\,d\xi }$
and
${\displaystyle {\frac {\partial y(x,0)}{\partial t}}=\int {\bigl \{}s\_{+}(\xi )-s\_{-}(\xi ){\bigr \}}i2\pi \xi e^{i2\pi \xi x+0}\,d\xi .}$${\displaystyle {\frac {\partial y(x,0)}{\partial t}}=\int {\bigl \{}s\_{+}(\xi )-s\_{-}(\xi ){\bigr \}}i2\pi \xi e^{i2\pi \xi x+0}\,d\xi .}$

Now, as before, applying the one-variable Fourier transformation in the variable x to these functions of x yields two equations in the two unknown distributions *s*Вұ (which can be taken to be ordinary functions if the boundary conditions are *L*1 or *L*2).

From a calculational point of view, the drawback of course is that one must first calculate the Fourier transforms of the boundary conditions, then assemble the solution from these, and then calculate an inverse Fourier transform. Closed form formulas are rare, except when there is some geometric symmetry that can be exploited, and the numerical calculations are difficult because of the oscillatory nature of the integrals, which makes convergence slow and hard to estimate. For practical calculations, other methods are often used.

#### Nonlinear Fourier transform

The twentieth century has seen application of these methods to all linear partial differential equations with polynomial coefficients as well as an extension to certain classes of [nonlinear partial differential equations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nonlinear_partial_differential_equation "Nonlinear partial differential equation"). Specifically, nonlinear evolution equations (i.e. those equations that describe how a particular quantity evolves in time from a specified initial state) that can be associated with linear eigenvalue problems whose eigenvalues are integrals of the nonlinear equations. As it may be considered an extension of Fourier analysis to nonlinear problems, the solution method is called the **nonlinear Fourier transform** (or **[inverse scattering transform](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inverse_scattering_transform "Inverse scattering transform")**) method.

### Fourier-transform spectroscopy

The Fourier transform is also used in [nuclear magnetic resonance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nuclear_magnetic_resonance "Nuclear magnetic resonance") (NMR) and in other kinds of [spectroscopy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spectroscopy "Spectroscopy"), e.g. infrared ([FTIR](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fourier-transform_infrared_spectroscopy "Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy")). In NMR an exponentially shaped free induction decay (FID) signal is acquired in the time domain and Fourier-transformed to a Lorentzian line-shape in the frequency domain. The Fourier transform is also used in [magnetic resonance imaging](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magnetic_resonance_imaging "Magnetic resonance imaging") (MRI) and [mass spectrometry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mass_spectrometry "Mass spectrometry").

### Quantum mechanics

The Fourier transform is useful in [quantum mechanics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_mechanics "Quantum mechanics") in at least two different ways. To begin with, the basic conceptual structure of quantum mechanics postulates the existence of pairs of [complementary variables](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Complementary_variables "Complementary variables"), connected by the [Heisenberg uncertainty principle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heisenberg_uncertainty_principle "Heisenberg uncertainty principle"). For example, in one dimension, the spatial variable q of, say, a particle, can only be measured by the quantum mechanical "[position operator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Position_operator "Position operator")" at the cost of losing information about the momentum p of the particle. Therefore, the physical state of the particle can either be described by a function, called "the wave function", of q or by a function of p but not by a function of both variables. The variable p is called the conjugate variable to q.

In classical mechanics, the physical state of a particle (existing in one dimension, for simplicity of exposition) would be given by assigning definite values to both p and q simultaneously. Thus, the set of all possible physical states is the two-dimensional real vector space with a p-axis and a q-axis called the [phase space](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phase_space "Phase space"). In contrast, quantum mechanics chooses a polarisation of this space in the sense that it picks a subspace of one-half the dimension, for example, the q-axis alone, but instead of considering only points, takes the set of all complex-valued "wave functions" on this axis. Nevertheless, choosing the p-axis is an equally valid polarisation, yielding a different representation of the set of possible physical states of the particle. Both representations of the wavefunction are related by a Fourier transform, such that
${\displaystyle \varphi (p)=\int dq\,\psi (q)e^{-ipq/h},}$${\displaystyle \varphi (p)=\int dq\,\psi (q)e^{-ipq/h},}$
or, equivalently,
${\displaystyle \psi (q)=\int dp\,\varphi (p)e^{ipq/h}.}$${\displaystyle \psi (q)=\int dp\,\varphi (p)e^{ipq/h}.}$

Physically realisable states are *L*2, and so by the [Plancherel theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plancherel_theorem "Plancherel theorem"), their Fourier transforms are also *L*2. (Note that since q is in units of distance and p is in units of momentum, the presence of the Planck constant in the exponent makes the exponent [dimensionless](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nondimensionalization "Nondimensionalization"), as it should be.)

Therefore, the Fourier transform can be used to pass from one way of representing the state of the particle, by a wave function of position, to another way of representing the state of the particle: by a wave function of momentum. Infinitely many different polarisations are possible, and all are equally valid. Being able to transform states from one representation to another by the Fourier transform is not only convenient but also the underlying reason of the Heisenberg [uncertainty principle](#Uncertainty_principle).

The other use of the Fourier transform in both quantum mechanics and [quantum field theory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_field_theory "Quantum field theory") is to solve the applicable wave equation. In non-relativistic quantum mechanics, the [SchrГ¶dinger equation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Schr%C3%B6dinger_equation "SchrГ¶dinger equation") for a time-varying wave function in one-dimension, not subject to external forces, is
${\displaystyle -{\frac {\partial ^{2}}{\partial x^{2}}}\psi (x,t)=i{\frac {h}{2\pi }}{\frac {\partial }{\partial t}}\psi (x,t).}$${\displaystyle -{\frac {\partial ^{2}}{\partial x^{2}}}\psi (x,t)=i{\frac {h}{2\pi }}{\frac {\partial }{\partial t}}\psi (x,t).}$

This is the same as the heat equation except for the presence of the imaginary unit i. Fourier methods can be used to solve this equation.

In the presence of a potential, given by the potential energy function *V*(*x*), the equation becomes
${\displaystyle -{\frac {\partial ^{2}}{\partial x^{2}}}\psi (x,t)+V(x)\psi (x,t)=i{\frac {h}{2\pi }}{\frac {\partial }{\partial t}}\psi (x,t).}$${\displaystyle -{\frac {\partial ^{2}}{\partial x^{2}}}\psi (x,t)+V(x)\psi (x,t)=i{\frac {h}{2\pi }}{\frac {\partial }{\partial t}}\psi (x,t).}$

The "elementary solutions", as we referred to them above, are the so-called "stationary states" of the particle, and Fourier's algorithm, as described above, can still be used to solve the boundary value problem of the future evolution of ПҲ given its values for *t* = 0. Neither of these approaches is of much practical use in quantum mechanics. Boundary value problems and the time-evolution of the wave function is not of much practical interest: it is the stationary states that are most important.

In relativistic quantum mechanics, the SchrГ¶dinger equation becomes a wave equation as was usual in classical physics, except that complex-valued waves are considered. A simple example, in the absence of interactions with other particles or fields, is the free one-dimensional KleinвҖ“GordonвҖ“SchrГ¶dingerвҖ“Fock equation, this time in dimensionless units,
${\displaystyle \left({\frac {\partial ^{2}}{\partial x^{2}}}+1\right)\psi (x,t)={\frac {\partial ^{2}}{\partial t^{2}}}\psi (x,t).}$${\displaystyle \left({\frac {\partial ^{2}}{\partial x^{2}}}+1\right)\psi (x,t)={\frac {\partial ^{2}}{\partial t^{2}}}\psi (x,t).}$

This is, from the mathematical point of view, the same as the wave equation of classical physics solved above (but with a complex-valued wave, which makes no difference in the methods). This is of great use in quantum field theory: each separate Fourier component of a wave can be treated as a separate harmonic oscillator and then quantized, a procedure known as "second quantization". Fourier methods have been adapted to also deal with non-trivial interactions.

Finally, the [number operator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_harmonic_oscillator#Ladder_operator_method "Quantum harmonic oscillator") of the [quantum harmonic oscillator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_harmonic_oscillator "Quantum harmonic oscillator") can be interpreted, for example via the [Mehler kernel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mehler_kernel#Physics_version "Mehler kernel"), as the [generator](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Symmetry_in_quantum_mechanics "Symmetry in quantum mechanics") of the [Fourier transform](#Eigenfunctions) вҒ ${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}}$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}}$вҒ .

### Signal processing

The Fourier transform is used for the spectral analysis of time-series. The subject of statistical signal processing does not, however, usually apply the Fourier transformation to the signal itself. Even if a real signal is indeed transient, it has been found in practice advisable to model a signal by a function (or, alternatively, a stochastic process) that is stationary in the sense that its characteristic properties are constant over all time. The Fourier transform of such a function does not exist in the usual sense, and it has been found more useful for the analysis of signals to instead take the Fourier transform of its autocorrelation function.

The autocorrelation function R of a function f is defined by
${\displaystyle R\_{f}(\tau )=\lim \_{T\rightarrow \infty }{\frac {1}{2T}}\int \_{-T}^{T}f(t)f(t+\tau )\,dt.}$${\displaystyle R\_{f}(\tau )=\lim \_{T\rightarrow \infty }{\frac {1}{2T}}\int \_{-T}^{T}f(t)f(t+\tau )\,dt.}$

This function is a function of the time-lag П„ elapsing between the values of f to be correlated.

For most functions f that occur in practice, R is a bounded even function of the time-lag П„ and for typical noisy signals it turns out to be uniformly continuous with a maximum at *П„* = 0.

The autocorrelation function, more properly called the autocovariance function unless it is normalized in some appropriate fashion, measures the strength of the correlation between the values of f separated by a time lag. This is a way of searching for the correlation of f with its own past. It is useful even for other statistical tasks besides the analysis of signals. For example, if *f*(*t*) represents the temperature at time t, one expects a strong correlation with the temperature at a time lag of 24 hours.

It possesses a Fourier transform,
