${\displaystyle P\_{f}(\xi )=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }R\_{f}(\tau )e^{-i2\pi \xi \tau }\,d\tau .}$${\displaystyle P\_{f}(\xi )=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }R\_{f}(\tau )e^{-i2\pi \xi \tau }\,d\tau .}$

This Fourier transform is called the [power spectral density](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spectral_density#Power_spectral_density "Spectral density") function of f. (Unless all periodic components are first filtered out from f, this integral will diverge, but it is easy to filter out such periodicities.)

The power spectrum, as indicated by this density function P, measures the amount of variance contributed to the data by the frequency Оҫ. In electrical signals, the variance is proportional to the average power (energy per unit time), and so the power spectrum describes how much the different frequencies contribute to the average power of the signal. This process is called the spectral analysis of time-series and is analogous to the usual analysis of variance of data that is not a time-series ([ANOVA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ANOVA "ANOVA")).

Knowledge of which frequencies are "important" in this sense is crucial for the proper design of filters and for the proper evaluation of measuring apparatuses. It can also be useful for the scientific analysis of the phenomena responsible for producing the data.

The power spectrum of a signal can also be approximately measured directly by measuring the average power that remains in a signal after all the frequencies outside a narrow band have been filtered out.

Spectral analysis is carried out for visual signals as well. The power spectrum ignores all phase relations, which is good enough for many purposes, but for video signals other types of spectral analysis must also be employed, still using the Fourier transform as a tool.

## Other notations

Other common notations for ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )}$ include:
${\displaystyle {\tilde {f}}(\xi ),\ F(\xi ),\ {\mathcal {F}}\left(f\right)(\xi ),\ \left({\mathcal {F}}f\right)(\xi ),\ {\mathcal {F}}(f),\ {\mathcal {F}}\{f\},\ {\mathcal {F}}{\bigl (}f(t){\bigr )},\ {\mathcal {F}}{\bigl \{}f(t){\bigr \}}.}$${\displaystyle {\tilde {f}}(\xi ),\ F(\xi ),\ {\mathcal {F}}\left(f\right)(\xi ),\ \left({\mathcal {F}}f\right)(\xi ),\ {\mathcal {F}}(f),\ {\mathcal {F}}\{f\},\ {\mathcal {F}}{\bigl (}f(t){\bigr )},\ {\mathcal {F}}{\bigl \{}f(t){\bigr \}}.}$

In the sciences and engineering it is also common to make substitutions like these:
${\displaystyle \xi \rightarrow f,\quad x\rightarrow t,\quad f\rightarrow x,\quad {\widehat {f}}\rightarrow X.}$${\displaystyle \xi \rightarrow f,\quad x\rightarrow t,\quad f\rightarrow x,\quad {\widehat {f}}\rightarrow X.}$

So the transform pair ${\displaystyle f(x)\ {\stackrel {\mathcal {F}}{\Longleftrightarrow }}\ {\widehat {f}}(\xi )}$${\displaystyle f(x)\ {\stackrel {\mathcal {F}}{\Longleftrightarrow }}\ {\widehat {f}}(\xi )}$ can become ${\displaystyle x(t)\ {\stackrel {\mathcal {F}}{\Longleftrightarrow }}\ X(f)}$${\displaystyle x(t)\ {\stackrel {\mathcal {F}}{\Longleftrightarrow }}\ X(f)}$

A disadvantage of the capital letter notation is when expressing a transform such as ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\cdot g}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\cdot g}$ or вҒ ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}'}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}'}$вҒ , which become the more awkward ${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}\{f\cdot g\}}$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}\{f\cdot g\}}$ and вҒ ${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}\{f'\}}$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}\{f'\}}$вҒ .

In some contexts such as particle physics, the same symbol ${\displaystyle f}$${\displaystyle f}$ may be used for both for a function as well as it Fourier transform, with the two only distinguished by their [argument](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Argument_of_a_function "Argument of a function") I.e. ${\displaystyle f(k\_{1}+k\_{2})}$${\displaystyle f(k\_{1}+k\_{2})}$ would refer to the Fourier transform because of the momentum argument, while ${\displaystyle f(x\_{0}+\pi {\vec {r}})}$${\displaystyle f(x\_{0}+\pi {\vec {r}})}$ would refer to the original function because of the positional argument. Although tildes may be used as in ${\displaystyle {\tilde {f}}}$${\displaystyle {\tilde {f}}}$ to indicate Fourier transforms, tildes may also be used to indicate a modification of a quantity with a more [Lorentz invariant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lorentz_invariant "Lorentz invariant") form, such as вҒ ${\displaystyle {\tilde {dk}}={\frac {dk}{(2\pi )^{3}2\omega }}}$${\displaystyle {\tilde {dk}}={\frac {dk}{(2\pi )^{3}2\omega }}}$вҒ , so care must be taken. Similarly, ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}}$ often denotes the [Hilbert transform](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hilbert_transform "Hilbert transform") of вҒ ${\displaystyle f}$${\displaystyle f}$вҒ .

The interpretation of the complex function *fМӮ*(*Оҫ*) may be aided by expressing it in [polar coordinate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polar_coordinate "Polar coordinate") form
${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )=A(\xi )e^{i\varphi (\xi )}}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi )=A(\xi )e^{i\varphi (\xi )}}$
in terms of the two real functions *A*(*Оҫ*) and *ПҶ*(*Оҫ*) where:
${\displaystyle A(\xi )=\left|{\widehat {f}}(\xi )\right|,}$${\displaystyle A(\xi )=\left|{\widehat {f}}(\xi )\right|,}$
is the [amplitude](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amplitude "Amplitude") and
${\displaystyle \varphi (\xi )=\arg \left({\widehat {f}}(\xi )\right),}$${\displaystyle \varphi (\xi )=\arg \left({\widehat {f}}(\xi )\right),}$
is the [phase](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phase_(waves) "Phase (waves)") (see *[Arg](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arg_(mathematics) "Arg (mathematics)")*).

Then the inverse transform can be written:
${\displaystyle f(x)=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }A(\xi )\ e^{i{\bigl (}2\pi \xi x+\varphi (\xi ){\bigr )}}\,d\xi ,}$${\displaystyle f(x)=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }A(\xi )\ e^{i{\bigl (}2\pi \xi x+\varphi (\xi ){\bigr )}}\,d\xi ,}$
which is a recombination of all the frequency components of *f*(*x*). Each component is a complex [sinusoid](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sinusoid "Sinusoid") of the form *e*2ПҖ*ixОҫ* whose amplitude is *A*(*Оҫ*) and whose initial [phase angle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phase_(waves) "Phase (waves)") (at *x* = 0) is *ПҶ*(*Оҫ*).

The Fourier transform may be thought of as a mapping on function spaces. This mapping is here denoted F and F(*f*) is used to denote the Fourier transform of the function f. This mapping is linear, which means that F can also be seen as a linear transformation on the function space and implies that the standard notation in linear algebra of applying a linear transformation to a vector (here the function *f*) can be used to write F *f* instead of F(*f*). Since the result of applying the Fourier transform is again a function, we can be interested in the value of this function evaluated at the value Оҫ for its variable, and this is denoted either as F *f*(*Оҫ*) or as (F *f*)(*Оҫ*). Notice that in the former case, it is implicitly understood that F is applied first to f and then the resulting function is evaluated at Оҫ, not the other way around.

In mathematics and various applied sciences, it is often necessary to distinguish between a function f and the value of f when its variable equals x, denoted *f*(*x*). This means that a notation like F(*f*(*x*)) formally can be interpreted as the Fourier transform of the values of f at x. Despite this flaw, the previous notation appears frequently, often when a particular function or a function of a particular variable is to be transformed. For example,
${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}{\bigl (}\operatorname {rect} (x){\bigr )}=\operatorname {sinc} (\xi )}$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}{\bigl (}\operatorname {rect} (x){\bigr )}=\operatorname {sinc} (\xi )}$
is sometimes used to express that the Fourier transform of a [rectangular function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rectangular_function "Rectangular function") is a [sinc function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sinc_function "Sinc function"), or
${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}{\bigl (}f(x+x\_{0}){\bigr )}={\mathcal {F}}{\bigl (}f(x){\bigr )}\,e^{i2\pi x\_{0}\xi }}$${\displaystyle {\mathcal {F}}{\bigl (}f(x+x\_{0}){\bigr )}={\mathcal {F}}{\bigl (}f(x){\bigr )}\,e^{i2\pi x\_{0}\xi }}$
is used to express the shift property of the Fourier transform.

Notice, that the last example is only correct under the assumption that the transformed function is a function of x, not of *x*0.

As discussed above, the [characteristic function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Characteristic_function_(probability_theory) "Characteristic function (probability theory)") of a random variable is the same as the [FourierвҖ“Stieltjes transform](#FourierвҖ“Stieltjes_transform) of its distribution measure, but in this context it is typical to take a different convention for the constants. Typically characteristic function is defined
${\displaystyle E\left(e^{it\cdot X}\right)=\int e^{it\cdot x}\,d\mu \_{X}(x).}$${\displaystyle E\left(e^{it\cdot X}\right)=\int e^{it\cdot x}\,d\mu \_{X}(x).}$

As in the case of the "non-unitary angular frequency" convention above, the factor of 2ПҖ appears in neither the normalizing constant nor the exponent. Unlike any of the conventions appearing above, this convention takes the opposite sign in the exponent.

## Computation methods

The appropriate computation method largely depends how the original mathematical function is represented and the desired form of the output function. In this section we consider both functions of a continuous variable, вҒ ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$вҒ , and functions of a discrete variable (i.e. ordered pairs of ${\displaystyle x}$${\displaystyle x}$ and ${\displaystyle f}$${\displaystyle f}$ values). For discrete-valued вҒ ${\displaystyle x}$${\displaystyle x}$вҒ , the transform integral becomes a summation of sinusoids, which is still a continuous function of frequency (вҒ ${\displaystyle \xi }$${\displaystyle \xi }$вҒ  or вҒ ${\displaystyle \omega }$${\displaystyle \omega }$вҒ ). When the sinusoids are harmonically related (i.e. when the ${\displaystyle x}$${\displaystyle x}$-values are spaced at integer multiples of an interval), the transform is called [discrete-time Fourier transform](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Discrete-time_Fourier_transform "Discrete-time Fourier transform") (DTFT).

### Discrete Fourier transforms and fast Fourier transforms

Sampling the DTFT at equally-spaced values of frequency is the most common modern method of computation. Efficient procedures, depending on the frequency resolution needed, are described at [Discrete-time Fourier transform В§В Sampling the DTFT](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Discrete-time_Fourier_transform#Sampling_the_DTFT "Discrete-time Fourier transform"). The [discrete Fourier transform](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Discrete_Fourier_transform "Discrete Fourier transform") (DFT), used there, is usually computed by a [fast Fourier transform](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fast_Fourier_transform "Fast Fourier transform") (FFT) algorithm.

### Symbolic integration of closed-form functions

Tables of [closed-form](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Closed-form_expression "Closed-form expression") Fourier transforms, such as [В§В Square-integrable functions, one-dimensional](#Square-integrable_functions,_one-dimensional) and [В§В Table of discrete-time Fourier transforms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Discrete-time_Fourier_transform#Table_of_discrete-time_Fourier_transforms "Discrete-time Fourier transform"), are created by mathematically evaluating the Fourier analysis integral (or summation) into another closed-form function of frequency (вҒ ${\displaystyle \xi }$${\displaystyle \xi }$вҒ  or вҒ ${\displaystyle \omega }$${\displaystyle \omega }$вҒ ). When mathematically possible, this provides a transform for a continuum of frequency values.

Many computer algebra systems such as [Matlab](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Matlab "Matlab") and [Mathematica](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematica "Mathematica") that are capable of [symbolic integration](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Symbolic_integration "Symbolic integration") are capable of computing Fourier transforms symbolically.
<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:Edit_summary>

### Numerical integration of closed-form continuous functions

Discrete sampling of the Fourier transform can also be done by [numerical integration](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Numerical_integration "Numerical integration") of the definition at each value of frequency for which transform is desired. The numerical integration approach works on a much broader class of functions than the analytic approach.

### Numerical integration of a series of ordered pairs

If the input function is a series of ordered pairs, numerical integration reduces to just a summation over the set of data pairs. The DTFT is a common subcase of this more general situation.

## Tables of important Fourier transforms

The following tables record some closed-form Fourier transforms. For functions *f*(*x*) and *g*(*x*) denote their Fourier transforms by *fМӮ* and *Дқ*. Only the three most common conventions are included. It may be useful to notice that entry 105 gives a relationship between the Fourier transform of a function and the original function, which can be seen as relating the Fourier transform and its inverse.

### Functional relationships, one-dimensional

The Fourier transforms in this table may be found in [ErdГ©lyi (1954)](#CITEREFErdГ©lyi1954) or [Kammler (2000](#CITEREFKammler2000), appendix).

|  | Function | Fourier transform   unitary, ordinary frequency | Fourier transform   unitary, angular frequency | Fourier transform   non-unitary, angular frequency | Remarks |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$ | ${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}&{\widehat {f}}(\xi )\triangleq {\widehat {f}}\_{1}(\xi )\\&=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)e^{-i2\pi \xi x}\,dx\end{aligned}}}$${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}&{\widehat {f}}(\xi )\triangleq {\widehat {f}}\_{1}(\xi )\\&=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)e^{-i2\pi \xi x}\,dx\end{aligned}}}$ | ${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}&{\widehat {f}}(\omega )\triangleq {\widehat {f}}\_{2}(\omega )\\&={\frac {1}{\sqrt {2\pi }}}\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)e^{-i\omega x}\,dx\end{aligned}}}$${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}&{\widehat {f}}(\omega )\triangleq {\widehat {f}}\_{2}(\omega )\\&={\frac {1}{\sqrt {2\pi }}}\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)e^{-i\omega x}\,dx\end{aligned}}}$ | ${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}&{\widehat {f}}(\omega )\triangleq {\widehat {f}}\_{3}(\omega )\\&=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)e^{-i\omega x}\,dx\end{aligned}}}$${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}&{\widehat {f}}(\omega )\triangleq {\widehat {f}}\_{3}(\omega )\\&=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)e^{-i\omega x}\,dx\end{aligned}}}$ | Definitions |
| 101 | ${\displaystyle a\,f(x)+b\,g(x)}$${\displaystyle a\,f(x)+b\,g(x)}$ | ${\displaystyle a\,{\widehat {f}}(\xi )+b\,{\widehat {g}}(\xi )}$${\displaystyle a\,{\widehat {f}}(\xi )+b\,{\widehat {g}}(\xi )}$ | ${\displaystyle a\,{\widehat {f}}(\omega )+b\,{\widehat {g}}(\omega )}$${\displaystyle a\,{\widehat {f}}(\omega )+b\,{\widehat {g}}(\omega )}$ | ${\displaystyle a\,{\widehat {f}}(\omega )+b\,{\widehat {g}}(\omega )}$${\displaystyle a\,{\widehat {f}}(\omega )+b\,{\widehat {g}}(\omega )}$ | Linearity |
| 102 | ${\displaystyle f(x-a)}$${\displaystyle f(x-a)}$ | ${\displaystyle e^{-i2\pi \xi a}{\widehat {f}}(\xi )}$${\displaystyle e^{-i2\pi \xi a}{\widehat {f}}(\xi )}$ | ${\displaystyle e^{-ia\omega }{\widehat {f}}(\omega )}$${\displaystyle e^{-ia\omega }{\widehat {f}}(\omega )}$ | ${\displaystyle e^{-ia\omega }{\widehat {f}}(\omega )}$${\displaystyle e^{-ia\omega }{\widehat {f}}(\omega )}$ | Shift in time domain |
| 103 | ${\displaystyle f(x)e^{iax}}$${\displaystyle f(x)e^{iax}}$ | ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\left(\xi -{\frac {a}{2\pi }}\right)}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\left(\xi -{\frac {a}{2\pi }}\right)}$ | ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\omega -a)}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\omega -a)}$ | ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\omega -a)}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\omega -a)}$ | Shift in frequency domain, dual of 102 |
| 104 | ${\displaystyle f(ax)}$${\displaystyle f(ax)}$ | ${\displaystyle {\frac {1}{|a|}}{\widehat {f}}\left({\frac {\xi }{a}}\right)}$${\displaystyle {\frac {1}{|a|}}{\widehat {f}}\left({\frac {\xi }{a}}\right)}$ | ${\displaystyle {\frac {1}{|a|}}{\widehat {f}}\left({\frac {\omega }{a}}\right)}$${\displaystyle {\frac {1}{|a|}}{\widehat {f}}\left({\frac {\omega }{a}}\right)}$ | ${\displaystyle {\frac {1}{|a|}}{\widehat {f}}\left({\frac {\omega }{a}}\right)}$${\displaystyle {\frac {1}{|a|}}{\widehat {f}}\left({\frac {\omega }{a}}\right)}$ | Scaling in the time domain. If |*a*| is large, then *f*(*ax*) is concentrated around 0 and ${\textstyle {\frac {1}{|a|}}{\widehat {f}}\left({\frac {\omega }{a}}\right)}$${\textstyle {\frac {1}{|a|}}{\widehat {f}}\left({\frac {\omega }{a}}\right)}$ spreads out and flattens. |
| 105 | ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\_{n}(x)}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\_{n}(x)}$ | ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\_{1}(x)\ {\stackrel {{\mathcal {F}}\_{1}}{\longleftrightarrow }}\ f(-\xi )}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\_{1}(x)\ {\stackrel {{\mathcal {F}}\_{1}}{\longleftrightarrow }}\ f(-\xi )}$ | ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\_{2}(x)\ {\stackrel {{\mathcal {F}}\_{2}}{\longleftrightarrow }}\ f(-\omega )}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\_{2}(x)\ {\stackrel {{\mathcal {F}}\_{2}}{\longleftrightarrow }}\ f(-\omega )}$ | ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\_{3}(x)\ {\stackrel {{\mathcal {F}}\_{3}}{\longleftrightarrow }}\ 2\pi f(-\omega )}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}\_{3}(x)\ {\stackrel {{\mathcal {F}}\_{3}}{\longleftrightarrow }}\ 2\pi f(-\omega )}$ | The same transform is applied twice, but *x* replaces the frequency variable (*Оҫ* or *Пү*) after the first transform. |

Note: ${\displaystyle \delta }$${\displaystyle \delta }$ is the [Dirac delta function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dirac_delta_function "Dirac delta function") and ${\displaystyle C}$${\displaystyle C}$ is the average ([DC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DC_component "DC component")) value of ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$ such that ${\textstyle \int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }(f(x)-C)\,dx=0}$${\textstyle \int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }(f(x)-C)\,dx=0}$ |

| 108 | ${\displaystyle (f\*g)(x)}$${\displaystyle (f\*g)(x)}$ | ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi ){\widehat {g}}(\xi )}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi ){\widehat {g}}(\xi )}$ | ${\displaystyle {\sqrt {2\pi }}\ {\widehat {f}}(\omega ){\widehat {g}}(\omega )}$${\displaystyle {\sqrt {2\pi }}\ {\widehat {f}}(\omega ){\widehat {g}}(\omega )}$ | ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\omega ){\widehat {g}}(\omega )}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\omega ){\widehat {g}}(\omega )}$ | The notation *f* вҲ— *g* denotes the [convolution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Convolution "Convolution") of f and g вҖ“ this rule is the [convolution theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Convolution_theorem "Convolution theorem") |

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | ${\displaystyle f(x)}$${\displaystyle f(x)}$ | ${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}&{\widehat {f}}(\xi )\triangleq {\widehat {f}}\_{1}(\xi )\\&=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)e^{-i2\pi \xi x}\,dx\end{aligned}}}$${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}&{\widehat {f}}(\xi )\triangleq {\widehat {f}}\_{1}(\xi )\\&=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)e^{-i2\pi \xi x}\,dx\end{aligned}}}$ | ${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}&{\widehat {f}}(\omega )\triangleq {\widehat {f}}\_{2}(\omega )\\&={\frac {1}{\sqrt {2\pi }}}\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)e^{-i\omega x}\,dx\end{aligned}}}$${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}&{\widehat {f}}(\omega )\triangleq {\widehat {f}}\_{2}(\omega )\\&={\frac {1}{\sqrt {2\pi }}}\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)e^{-i\omega x}\,dx\end{aligned}}}$ | ${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}&{\widehat {f}}(\omega )\triangleq {\widehat {f}}\_{3}(\omega )\\&=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)e^{-i\omega x}\,dx\end{aligned}}}$${\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}&{\widehat {f}}(\omega )\triangleq {\widehat {f}}\_{3}(\omega )\\&=\int \_{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x)e^{-i\omega x}\,dx\end{aligned}}}$ | Definitions |
| 201 | ${\displaystyle \operatorname {rect} (ax)}$${\displaystyle \operatorname {rect} (ax)}$ | ${\displaystyle {\frac {1}{|a|}}\,\operatorname {sinc} \left({\frac {\xi }{a}}\right)}$${\displaystyle {\frac {1}{|a|}}\,\operatorname {sinc} \left({\frac {\xi }{a}}\right)}$ | ${\displaystyle {\frac {1}{\sqrt {2\pi a^{2}}}}\,\operatorname {sinc} \left({\frac {\omega }{2\pi a}}\right)}$${\displaystyle {\frac {1}{\sqrt {2\pi a^{2}}}}\,\operatorname {sinc} \left({\frac {\omega }{2\pi a}}\right)}$ | ${\displaystyle {\frac {1}{|a|}}\,\operatorname {sinc} \left({\frac {\omega }{2\pi a}}\right)}$${\displaystyle {\frac {1}{|a|}}\,\operatorname {sinc} \left({\frac {\omega }{2\pi a}}\right)}$ | The [rectangular pulse](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rectangular_function "Rectangular function") and the *normalized* [sinc function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sinc_function "Sinc function"), here defined as sinc(*x*) = вҒ sin(ПҖ*x*)/ПҖ*x*вҒ  |
| 202 | ${\displaystyle \operatorname {sinc} (ax)}$${\displaystyle \operatorname {sinc} (ax)}$ | ${\displaystyle {\frac {1}{|a|}}\,\operatorname {rect} \left({\frac {\xi }{a}}\right)}$${\displaystyle {\frac {1}{|a|}}\,\operatorname {rect} \left({\frac {\xi }{a}}\right)}$ | ${\displaystyle {\frac {1}{\sqrt {2\pi a^{2}}}}\,\operatorname {rect} \left({\frac {\omega }{2\pi a}}\right)}$${\displaystyle {\frac {1}{\sqrt {2\pi a^{2}}}}\,\operatorname {rect} \left({\frac {\omega }{2\pi a}}\right)}$ | ${\displaystyle {\frac {1}{|a|}}\,\operatorname {rect} \left({\frac {\omega }{2\pi a}}\right)}$${\displaystyle {\frac {1}{|a|}}\,\operatorname {rect} \left({\frac {\omega }{2\pi a}}\right)}$ | Dual of rule 201. The [rectangular function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rectangular_function "Rectangular function") is an ideal [low-pass filter](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Low-pass_filter "Low-pass filter"), and the [sinc function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sinc_function "Sinc function") is the [non-causal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anticausal_system "Anticausal system") impulse response of such a filter. The [sinc function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sinc_function "Sinc function") is defined here as sinc(*x*) = вҒ sin(ПҖ*x*)/ПҖ*x*вҒ . |

It is necessary to use a finite part integral when testing вҒ 1/|*Оҫ*|вҒ  or вҒ 1/|*Пү*|вҒ against [Schwartz functions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Schwartz_functions "Schwartz functions"). | 320 | ${\displaystyle \left(\mp ix\right)^{-\alpha }}$${\displaystyle \left(\mp ix\right)^{-\alpha }}$ | ${\displaystyle {\frac {\left(2\pi \right)^{\alpha }}{\Gamma \left(\alpha \right)}}u\left(\pm \xi \right)\left(\pm \xi \right)^{\alpha -1}}$${\displaystyle {\frac {\left(2\pi \right)^{\alpha }}{\Gamma \left(\alpha \right)}}u\left(\pm \xi \right)\left(\pm \xi \right)^{\alpha -1}}$ | ${\displaystyle {\frac {\sqrt {2\pi }}{\Gamma \left(\alpha \right)}}u\left(\pm \omega \right)\left(\pm \omega \right)^{\alpha -1}}$${\displaystyle {\frac {\sqrt {2\pi }}{\Gamma \left(\alpha \right)}}u\left(\pm \omega \right)\left(\pm \omega \right)^{\alpha -1}}$ | ${\displaystyle {\frac {2\pi }{\Gamma \left(\alpha \right)}}u\left(\pm \omega \right)\left(\pm \omega \right)^{\alpha -1}}$${\displaystyle {\frac {2\pi }{\Gamma \left(\alpha \right)}}u\left(\pm \omega \right)\left(\pm \omega \right)^{\alpha -1}}$ | This formula is valid for 0 < *Оұ* < 1. Use differentiation to derive formula for higher exponents. u is the Heaviside function. |

## Notes

1. Sentence structure is often sufficient to distinguish the intended meaning: for example, "Apply the Fourier transform to [an input]" refers to the operation, whereas "The Fourier transform of [an input]" refers to its output.
2. Depending on the application a [Lebesgue integral](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lebesgue_integral "Lebesgue integral"), [distributional](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Distribution_(mathematics) "Distribution (mathematics)"), or other approach may be most appropriate.
3. [Vretblad (2000)](#CITEREFVretblad2000) provides solid justification for these formal procedures without going too deeply into [functional analysis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Functional_analysis "Functional analysis") or the [theory of distributions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Distribution_(mathematics) "Distribution (mathematics)").
4. In [relativistic quantum mechanics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Relativistic_quantum_mechanics "Relativistic quantum mechanics") one encounters vector-valued Fourier transforms of multi-component wave functions. In [quantum field theory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_field_theory "Quantum field theory"), operator-valued Fourier transforms of operator-valued functions of spacetime are in frequent use, see for example [Greiner & Reinhardt (1996)](#CITEREFGreinerReinhardt1996).
5. A possible source of confusion is the [frequency-shifting property](#Frequency_shifting); i.e. the transform of function ${\displaystyle f(x)e^{-i2\pi \xi \_{0}x}}$${\displaystyle f(x)e^{-i2\pi \xi \_{0}x}}$ is вҒ ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi +\xi \_{0})}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi +\xi \_{0})}$вҒ . The value of this function at ${\displaystyle \xi =0}$${\displaystyle \xi =0}$ is вҒ ${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi \_{0})}$${\displaystyle {\widehat {f}}(\xi \_{0})}$вҒ , meaning that a frequency ${\displaystyle \xi \_{0}}$${\displaystyle \xi \_{0}}$ has been shifted to zero (also see *[Negative frequency В§В Simplifying the Fourier transform](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Negative_frequency#Simplifying_the_Fourier_transform "Negative frequency")*).
6. The operator ${\textstyle U\left({\frac {1}{2\pi }}{\frac {d}{dx}}\right)}$${\textstyle U\left({\frac {1}{2\pi }}{\frac {d}{dx}}\right)}$ is defined by replacing ${\displaystyle x}$${\displaystyle x}$ by ${\textstyle {\frac {1}{2\pi }}{\frac {d}{dx}}}$${\textstyle {\frac {1}{2\pi }}{\frac {d}{dx}}}$ in the [Taylor expansion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taylor_series "Taylor series") of вҒ ${\displaystyle U(x)}$${\displaystyle U(x)}$вҒ .
7. More generally, one can take a sequence of functions that are in the intersection of *L*1 and *L*2 and that converges to f in the *L*2-norm, and define the Fourier transform of f as the *L*2 -limit of the Fourier transforms of these functions.
8. Up to an imaginary constant factor whose magnitude depends on what Fourier transform convention is used.
9. For example, to compute the Fourier transform of cos(6ПҖ*t*) *e*вҲ’ПҖ*t*2 one might enter the command `integrate cos(6*pi*t) exp(вҲ’pi*t^2) exp(-i*2*pi*f*t) from -inf to inf` into [Wolfram Alpha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wolfram_Alpha "Wolfram Alpha"). The direct command `fourier transform of cos(6*pi*t) exp(вҲ’pi*t^2)` would also work for Wolfram Alpha, although the options for the convention (see *[В§В Other conventions](#Other_conventions)*) must be changed away from the default option, which is actually equivalent to `integrate cos(6*pi*t) exp(вҲ’pi*t^2) exp(i*omega*t) /sqrt(2*pi) from -inf to inf`.
10. In [Gelfand & Shilov 1964](#CITEREFGelfandShilov1964), p.В 363, with the non-unitary conventions of this table, the transform of ${\displaystyle |\mathbf {x} |^{\lambda }}$${\displaystyle |\mathbf {x} |^{\lambda }}$ is given to be вҒ ${\displaystyle \textstyle 2^{\lambda +n}\pi ^{{\tfrac {1}{2}}n}{\Gamma ({\frac {\lambda +n}{2}})}/{\Gamma (-{\frac {\lambda }{2}})}\vert {\boldsymbol {\omega }}\vert ^{-\lambda -n}}$${\displaystyle \textstyle 2^{\lambda +n}\pi ^{{\tfrac {1}{2}}n}{\Gamma ({\frac {\lambda +n}{2}})}/{\Gamma (-{\frac {\lambda }{2}})}\vert {\boldsymbol {\omega }}\vert ^{-\lambda -n}}$вҒ  from which this follows, with вҒ ${\displaystyle \lambda =-\alpha }$${\displaystyle \lambda =-\alpha }$вҒ .
